content: (2) Any investment made under sub-regulation (1) shall be in accordance with the provisions of these regulations, investment objective of the restricted scheme and disclosures made in the placement memorandum. (3) In addition to the above, a close ended scheme may invest up to twenty per cent
summary: Section 34(2)-(3) requires all investments to align with regulations, investment objectives, and placement memorandum disclosures, while limiting close-ended scheme investments to twenty percent in specified assets.

content: (20%) of the corpus in physical assets such as real estate, bullion, art or any other physical asset as may be specified by the Authority.
summary: Section 34 limits investment in physical assets, including real estate, bullion, and art, to a maximum of 20% of the scheme's corpus, subject to Authority specifications.

content: (1) In case of an open-ended scheme, the maximum investment in unlisted securities should not exceed twenty-five per cent
summary: Section 35(1) mandates that open-ended schemes must limit investments in unlisted securities to a maximum of 25% of the scheme's corpus.

content: (25%) of the corpus of the scheme: Provided that in case of an open-ended fund of funds scheme, this requirement shall not be applicable if such scheme is investing in other open-ended scheme(s) which shall not have investment in unlisted securities in excess of twenty-five per cent
summary: Section 35(1) provides an exemption for open-ended fund of funds schemes from the 25% unlisted securities limit, provided the underlying schemes also adhere to the 25% threshold.

content: (25%) of their corpus. Provided further that the investments by an open-ended scheme in unlisted securities shall be undertaken only upon achieving the minimum corpus of USD 3 Million
summary: Section 35(1) stipulates that open-ended schemes may only invest in unlisted securities once the scheme has achieved a minimum corpus of USD 3 Million.

content: (2) The minimum size of corpus of the restricted schemes shall be USD 3 Million: Provided that an open-ended scheme may commence investment activities upon raising at least USD 1 Million in funds and shall achieve the minimum corpus of USD 3 Million within 12 months from the date of communication
summary: Section 35(2) sets the minimum corpus for restricted schemes at USD 3 Million, allowing open-ended schemes to commence operations at USD 1 Million provided the USD 3 Million threshold is met within 12 months.

content: within 12 months from the date of communication from the Authority that the placement memorandum of the scheme has been taken on record
summary: The 12-month period for achieving the minimum USD 3 Million corpus commences from the date the Authority confirms the scheme's placement memorandum has been taken on record.

content: Provided further that if a FME fails to achieve the minimum size of corpus of USD 3 Million within the specified time period, it shall have the option to extend the validity of the placement memorandum, wherein each such extension shall be for a period of six (6) months starting from the day after
summary: Section 35 allows a Fund Management Entity (FME) to extend the validity of a placement memorandum by six-month increments if the minimum USD 3 Million corpus is not achieved within the initial timeframe.

content: of six (6) months starting from the day after the expiry of the existing validity of the placement memorandum, by filing an application at such time when the placement memorandum is still valid, accompanied by a fee equal to- i) for the first extension, twenty-five per cent
summary: Extensions for placement memorandum validity must be filed before the current expiry date and require payment of a prescribed fee based on the fresh scheme filing fee.

content: (25%) of the applicable fee for filing of a fresh scheme, as may be prevalent at the time of such extension; and ii) for each subsequent extension, fifty per cent. (50%) of the applicable fee for filing of a fresh scheme, as may be prevalent at the time of such extension
summary: The extension fee for a restricted scheme is 25% of the fresh filing fee for the first extension and 50% of the fresh filing fee for each subsequent extension.

content: (3) Restricted schemes may invest in its associate subject to the prior approval of seventy-five per cent. (75%) investors in the scheme by value
summary: Section 35(3) permits restricted schemes to invest in associates, provided they obtain prior approval from investors representing at least 75% of the scheme's value.

content: (75%) investors in the scheme by value. (4) Restricted schemes shall not buy or sell securities from associates, other schemes of the FME or its associates, or an investor who has committed to invest at least fifty per cent
summary: Section 35(4) prohibits restricted schemes from trading securities with associates, other FME schemes, or investors committing at least 50% of the corpus, unless approved by 75% of investors by value.

content: (50%) of the corpus of the scheme, unless prior approval has been obtained from seventy-five per cent. (75%) investors in the scheme by value: Provided that the voting process shall exclude such investor(s) who have committed to invest at least fifty per cent
summary: Under Section 35, the voting process for approving restricted scheme transactions with major investors (50% commitment) must exclude the specific investor involved in the transaction.

content: (50%) of the corpus of the scheme and is buying or selling the securities, from or to, the scheme: Provided further that such approval from investors may not be required for a fund of funds scheme which has disclosed in its placement memorandum the details of the underlying scheme(s) wherein the
summary: Section 35 provides an exemption from investor approval requirements for fund of funds schemes that disclose underlying scheme details and FME associations in the placement memorandum.

content: details of the underlying scheme(s) wherein the investments are intended to be made and the nature of association, if any, that the FME has with the manager(s) of such underlying scheme(s)
summary: Section 35 requires placement memoranda for fund of funds to specify intended underlying investments and the nature of any association between the FME and the underlying scheme managers.

content: (5) A Restricted scheme filed with the Authority as a Category I AIF, Category II AIF or Category III AIF shall invest in accordance with such additional conditions as may be specified by the Authority.
summary: Section 35(5) mandates that restricted schemes registered as Category I, II, or III AIFs must comply with additional investment conditions specified by the Authority.

content: (1) The placement memorandum for restricted schemes shall clearly include disclosures regarding the investment objective, the targeted investors, proposed corpus, investment style or strategy, investment methodology, proposed tenure of the scheme, proposed fees and expenses, risk management
summary: Section 36(1) requires the placement memorandum for restricted schemes to disclose investment objectives, target investors, corpus, strategy, methodology, tenure, fees, and risk management practices.

content: proposed fees and expenses, risk management practices, KMPs of the FME and other relevant details of the FME and the scheme
summary: Section 36(1) mandates the disclosure of Key Management Personnel (KMP) of the FME and other relevant details concerning the FME and the scheme within the placement memorandum.

content: (2) Any material deviation or alteration to the fund strategy should be made with the consent of at least two-thirds (2/3rd) of investors by value
summary: Section 36(2) stipulates that any material deviation or alteration to the investment strategy of a restricted scheme requires the consent of at least two-thirds (2/3) of investors by value.

content: (3) The FME shall ensure that the NAV is disclosed to the investors at least on a monthly basis in case of an open-ended scheme and half-yearly in case of a close ended scheme within such time period as disclosed in the placement memorandum
summary: Section 36(3) requires FMEs to disclose the Net Asset Value (NAV) to investors monthly for open-ended schemes and half-yearly for close-ended schemes, as specified in the placement memorandum.

content: (4) The FME shall ensure that the portfolio under the scheme is disclosed to the investors at least on a quarterly basis within one month from the end of the quarter
summary: Section 36(4) mandates that FMEs disclose the scheme's portfolio to investors on a quarterly basis, no later than one month following the end of each quarter.

content: (5) Any other material disclosure as considered suitable by the FME or the fiduciaries shall be informed to the investors immediately. (6) The FME and the fiduciaries shall comply with the disclosure requirements as may be specified by the Authority.
summary: Section 36(5)-(6) mandates that the Fund Management Entity (FME) and fiduciaries must immediately disclose material information to investors and comply with all disclosure requirements specified by the Authority.

content: A restricted scheme may borrow funds or engage in leveraging activities, subject to the following conditions:- (a) The maximum leverage by the scheme, along with the methodology for calculation of leverage, shall be disclosed in the placement memorandum; (b) The leverage shall be exercised in
summary: Section 37(a) permits restricted schemes to borrow or leverage funds, provided the maximum leverage limit and calculation methodology are explicitly disclosed in the placement memorandum.

content: (b) The leverage shall be exercised in accordance with the disclosures in the placement memorandum and any deviation therefrom shall be subject to consent of two-thirds (2/3rd) of the investors by value; and (c) The FME intending to employ leverage shall have a comprehensive risk management
summary: Section 37(b)-(c) requires that any deviation from disclosed leverage limits necessitates consent from two-thirds of investors by value, and mandates that the FME maintain a comprehensive risk management framework.

content: shall have a comprehensive risk management framework appropriate to the size, complexity and risk profile of the fund.
summary: Section 37 requires the FME's risk management framework to be commensurate with the specific size, complexity, and risk profile of the fund.

content: (1) The FME and fiduciaries shall ensure compliance of investment valuation norms specified in the Sixth Schedule
summary: Section 38(1) mandates that the FME and fiduciaries strictly adhere to the investment valuation norms prescribed in the Sixth Schedule.

content: (2) In line with the investment valuation norms, the assets of the scheme shall be valued by an independent service provider, such as a fund administrator, a custodian, a credit rating agency registered with the Authority or a valuer registered with Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India or such
summary: Section 38(2) requires scheme assets to be valued by an independent service provider, such as a fund administrator, custodian, credit rating agency, or a valuer registered with the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India.

content: Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India or such other person as may be specified by the Authority: Provided that the above requirement shall not apply in case of a fund of funds scheme that invests in scheme(s) regulated by a financial sector regulator, directly or through a manager, in IFSC,
summary: Section 38 provides an exemption from the independent valuation requirement for fund of funds schemes that invest in schemes regulated by a financial sector regulator in IFSC, India, or foreign jurisdictions.

content: directly or through a manager, in IFSC, India or foreign jurisdiction(s), which are valued by any independent entity.
summary: Section 38 clarifies that the valuation exemption applies to fund of funds schemes where the underlying assets are already valued by an independent entity.

content: (1) FME shall compute the NAV of each restricted scheme at least on a monthly basis: Provided that in case of a close ended restricted scheme the computation of NAV shall take place at least half-yearly
summary: Section 39(1) mandates that the FME compute the Net Asset Value (NAV) of restricted schemes at least monthly, or at least half-yearly for close-ended restricted schemes.

content: (2) The procedure and methodology for calculating the NAV should be fully documented, and such documentation should be regularly verified and amended, if required.
summary: Section 39(2) requires the FME to fully document, regularly verify, and amend as necessary the procedures and methodologies used for calculating the NAV.

content: Under a restricted scheme, the FME or its associate shall invest :- (a) In case of a close ended scheme, (i) at least 2.5% of the targeted corpus and not exceeding 10% of the targeted corpus in a scheme with targeted corpus up to USD 30 Million; (ii) at least USD 750,000 and not exceeding 10% of
summary: Section 40 mandates that for close-ended restricted schemes, the FME or its associate must invest between 2.5% and 10% of the targeted corpus if the corpus is up to USD 30 Million.

content: at least USD 750,000 and not exceeding 10% of the targeted corpus in a scheme with targeted corpus more than USD 30 Million: (b) In case of an open-ended scheme, (i) at least 5% of the targeted corpus and not exceeding 10% of the targeted corpus in a scheme with targeted corpus less than USD 30
summary: For close-ended schemes with a targeted corpus exceeding USD 30 Million, the FME or its associate must invest between USD 750,000 and 10% of the corpus; for open-ended schemes under USD 30 Million, the requirement is 5% to 10%.

content: in a scheme with targeted corpus less than USD 30 Million; (ii) at least USD 1,500,000 and not exceeding 10% of the targeted corpus in a scheme with targeted corpus more than USD 30 Million: Provided that the contribution by the FME or its associate shall not be mandatory in case of relocated funds
summary: For open-ended schemes exceeding USD 30 Million, the FME or its associate must invest between USD 1,500,000 and 10% of the corpus, with an exemption for relocated funds from outside India to IFSC.

content: shall not be mandatory in case of relocated funds /schemes established or incorporated or registered outside India to IFSC: Provided further that ceiling of 10% shall not apply for restricted schemes if: (i) the FME and its associate investing in the scheme, are persons resident outside India and
summary: The 10% investment ceiling for restricted schemes is waived if the FME and its associates are persons resident outside India with no Indian resident ultimate beneficial owners.

content: scheme, are persons resident outside India and do not have any person resident in India as their ultimate beneficial owners; and (ii) not more than one-third of the corpus of the scheme is invested in an Investee Company and its associates
summary: The 10% ceiling waiver further requires that no more than one-third of the scheme's corpus is invested in an Investee Company and its associates.

content: (2) The said contribution in proportion to investor’s investment in the scheme shall be made by the FME or its associate within forty-five (45) days and maintained on ongoing basis: Provided that the period of forty-five (45) days may be extended subject to the satisfaction of the Authority
summary: Section 40(2) requires the FME or its associate to maintain the mandatory contribution on an ongoing basis, with the initial investment to be made within 45 days, subject to extension by the Authority.

content: (3) The said contribution, if brought in by FME, may be taken into consideration for the purpose of net-worth requirements as detailed under the Chapter II
summary: Section 40(3) permits the FME's mandatory contribution to be counted toward the net-worth requirements specified in Chapter II.

content: (4) The said contribution shall be exempted if :- (a) at least two-thirds (2/3 rd) of the investors in the scheme by value permits waiver of such contribution; (b) at least two-thirds (2/3 rd) of the investors in the scheme are accredited investors; or (c) The scheme is a fund of funds scheme
summary: Section 40(4) provides exemptions from the mandatory contribution if two-thirds of investors by value waive it, two-thirds are accredited investors, or the scheme is a fund of funds.

content: or (c) The scheme is a fund of funds scheme investing in scheme(s) with similar requirements.
summary: Section 40(4)(c) extends the mandatory contribution exemption to fund of funds schemes that invest in other schemes with similar regulatory requirements.

content: (1) A restricted scheme may co-invest in permissible investments under sub-regulation (1) of regulation 34 through a SPV in accordance with the framework specified by the Authority or through a segregated portfolio by issuing a separate class of units
summary: Section 41(1) allows restricted schemes to co-invest in permissible investments under regulation 34(1) via a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV) or a segregated portfolio with a separate class of units.

content: (2) The FME shall ensure that:- (a) The investments by such segregated portfolios shall, in no circumstances, be on terms more favourable than those offered to the common portfolio of the restricted scheme; and (b) Appropriate disclosures have been made in the placement memorandum regarding
summary: Section 41(2) mandates that FME segregated portfolio investments must not be on terms more favorable than the restricted scheme's common portfolio and requires appropriate placement memorandum disclosures.

content: been made in the placement memorandum regarding creation of segregated portfolio
summary: Section 41 requires that the creation of a segregated portfolio must be explicitly disclosed within the placement memorandum.

content: (3) A SPV mentioned in sub-regulation (1) may undertake leverage as disclosed in the placement memorandum.
summary: Section 41(3) permits a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV) to undertake leverage provided it is disclosed in the placement memorandum.

content: Retail Schemes are schemes that may be launched by Registered FMEs (Retail) for pooling money from all investors or a section of investors through an offer document for investment as per its stated investment objective in various permissible investments.
summary: Section 42 defines Retail Schemes as investment vehicles launched by Registered FMEs (Retail) to pool capital from investors for permissible investments as per stated objectives.

content: (1) No scheme shall be launched unless a draft offer document is filed with the Authority along with the applicable fees at least twenty-one (21) working days before the launch of the scheme
summary: Section 43(1) requires FMEs to file a draft offer document with the Authority at least twenty-one working days before launching any scheme, accompanied by applicable fees.

content: (2) The validity of the offer document for launch of the scheme shall be twelve (12) months from the date of communication from the Authority to the FME that the offer document has been taken on record
summary: Section 43(2) stipulates that an offer document remains valid for twelve months from the date the Authority communicates that it has been taken on record.

content: Explanation.- For the purpose of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the communication received by the FME from the Authority that the offer document has been taken on record, shall be construed as Certificate of Registration
summary: Section 43 clarifies that for the Income Tax Act, 1961, the Authority's communication confirming an offer document is taken on record serves as the Certificate of Registration.

content: (3) The FME shall ensure that the comments of the Authority are incorporated in the offer document prior to launch of the scheme. (4) The FME shall ensure that any material changes from the information disclosed in the draft offer document are informed to the Authority immediately
summary: Section 43(3) and (4) mandate that FMEs incorporate Authority comments into the offer document before launch and immediately report any material changes from the draft document.

content: (5) No retail scheme shall be filed with the Authority unless it has been approved by the fiduciaries.
summary: Section 43(5) prohibits the filing of any retail scheme with the Authority unless it has received prior approval from the fiduciaries.

content: Retail schemes shall have at least twenty (20) investors with no single investor investing more than twenty-five per cent. (25%) in a scheme and shall ensure compliance with this requirement within a maximum period of six (6) months from the closure of the offer.
summary: Section 44 requires retail schemes to maintain at least twenty investors, with no single investor exceeding a twenty-five percent stake, within six months of the offer closure.

content: (1) Retail schemes may be open-ended or close-ended. (2) The tenure of a close-ended scheme, which shall not be less than three (3) years, shall be disclosed in the offer document
summary: Section 45(1)-(2) mandates that retail schemes may be open-ended or close-ended, with close-ended schemes requiring a minimum tenure of three years disclosed in the offer document.

content: (3) The tenure of the close ended scheme may be extended up to two (2) years, subject to approval of two-thirds (2/3 rd) of the investors by value of their investment in the scheme and the approval of the Authority
summary: Section 45(3) permits the extension of a close-ended scheme's tenure by up to two years, contingent upon approval from two-thirds of investors by investment value and the Authority.

content: (4) Retail schemes shall be constituted in IFSC as Company or Trust under the applicable laws of India
summary: Section 45(4) requires retail schemes constituted in the IFSC to be structured as either a Company or a Trust under applicable Indian laws.

content: (5) Retail schemes may be launched for various investment strategies including for investment in Social Ventures, Infrastructure, towards ESG objectives, specific sectors (sectoral schemes), certain themes such as infrastructure (thematic schemes), certain asset class (equity schemes, debt schemes,
summary: Section 45(5) authorizes retail schemes to pursue diverse investment strategies, including Social Ventures, Infrastructure, ESG objectives, sectoral, thematic, equity, and debt schemes.

content: asset class (equity schemes, debt schemes, etc.) or a combination thereof or towards certain solution (retirement schemes, schemes for children education, etc.) subject to such terms and conditions as may be specified by the Authority.
summary: Section 45(5) further permits retail schemes to focus on specific solutions like retirement or education, subject to terms and conditions specified by the Authority.

content: (1) Subject to other provisions of these regulations, a retail scheme may invest only in the following instruments or entities in IFSC, India or foreign jurisdictions:- (a) Securities listed or to be listed or traded on stock exchanges; (b) Unlisted securities; (c) Money market instruments; (d)
summary: Section 46(1) restricts retail scheme investments to listed or unlisted securities, money market instruments, and debt securities within the IFSC, India, or foreign jurisdictions.

content: securities; (c) Money market instruments; (d) Debt securities; (e) Securitised debt instruments, which are either asset backed or mortgage-backed securities; (f) Units of other investment schemes subject to appropriate disclosure in the offer documents; (g) Derivatives including commodity
summary: Section 46(1) permits retail schemes to invest in securitised debt instruments (asset or mortgage-backed), units of other investment schemes, and derivatives, subject to offer document disclosures.

content: documents; (g) Derivatives including commodity derivatives only for the purpose of hedging subject to suitable disclosures in the offer document; or (h) Such other securities or financial products/ assets or instruments as specified by the Authority: Provided that pending deployment of monies, FME
summary: Section 46(1)(g) limits the use of derivatives, including commodity derivatives, to hedging purposes only, provided suitable disclosures are made in the offer document.

content: Provided that pending deployment of monies, FME may invest in certificates of deposit, units of investment schemes such as overnight, liquid or money market schemes, money market instruments, bank deposits or any other securities or financial assets or instruments as may be specified by the
summary: Section 46 allows FMEs to invest pending deployment of funds into certificates of deposit, liquid or money market schemes, bank deposits, or other instruments specified by the Authority.

content: assets or instruments as may be specified by the Authority
summary: Section 46 authorizes the Authority to specify additional securities, financial products, assets, or instruments for retail scheme investment.

content: (2) Any investment made under sub-regulation (1) shall be in accordance with the provisions of these regulations, investment objective of the retail scheme and disclosures in the offer document.
summary: Section 46(2) mandates that investments under sub-regulation (1) must comply with these regulations, the retail scheme's investment objective, and disclosures in the offer document.

content: (1) In case of an open-ended scheme, the maximum investment in unlisted securities should not exceed fifteen per cent
summary: Section 47(1) limits the maximum investment in unlisted securities for open-ended schemes to 15% of the total Assets Under Management (AUM).

content: (15%) of the total AUM of the scheme: Provided that this restriction shall not be applicable in case of investment in unlisted securities issued by an investment fund which is open-ended in nature, regulated by the concerned regulatory authority in its home jurisdiction, and is permitted for
summary: Section 47(1) provides an exemption to the 15% unlisted securities limit for investments in open-ended investment funds regulated and permitted for retail offering in their home jurisdiction.

content: in its home jurisdiction, and is permitted for offering to retail investors in its home jurisdiction
summary: This segment clarifies that the exemption from the 15% unlisted securities limit applies to investment funds authorized for retail distribution within their home jurisdiction.

content: (2) The minimum amount of investment by an investor in case of close-ended schemes investing more than fifteen per cent. (15%) in unlisted securities, shall be USD 10,000: Provided that a close-ended scheme shall not invest more than fifty per cent
summary: Section 47(2) mandates a minimum investment of USD 10,000 for close-ended schemes investing over 15% in unlisted securities, while capping total unlisted security exposure at 50%.

content: (50%) in unlisted securities: Provided further that the minimum amount of investment of USD 10,000 and the cap of fifty per cent
summary: This provision establishes the 50% cap on unlisted security investments for close-ended schemes and references the USD 10,000 minimum investment threshold.

content: (50%) shall not be applicable in case of investment by close-ended retail scheme in unlisted securities issued by an investment fund which is regulated by the concerned regulatory authority in its home jurisdiction, and is permitted for offering to retail investors in its home jurisdiction
summary: Section 47(2) exempts close-ended retail schemes from the USD 10,000 minimum investment and 50% unlisted security cap when investing in regulated, retail-offered investment funds.

content: (3) A Retail scheme shall not invest more than ten per cent. (10%) of its AUM in securities of a single company: Provided that a retail scheme may invest up to fifteen per cent
summary: Section 47(3) restricts retail schemes to a maximum 10% AUM investment in a single company, with a provision allowing up to 15% subject to fiduciary approval.

content: (15%) in a single company with the prior approval of the fiduciaries: Provided further that the limit on investment in single company in case of sectoral or thematic or Index schemes shall be the weightage of that company in the representative index, provided by an independent entity, that such
summary: For sectoral, thematic, or index schemes, the single-company investment limit is the higher of 15% or the company's weightage in the independent representative benchmark index.

content: provided by an independent entity, that such scheme intends to benchmark with, or 15%, whichever is higher: Provided also that fund of funds schemes shall be permitted to invest in other scheme(s) if such scheme(s) meets the requirement under this regulation
summary: This provision allows fund of funds schemes to invest in other schemes provided those underlying schemes comply with the requirements of Section 47.

content: (4) Retail schemes shall not invest more than twenty-five per cent. (25%) of its AUM in a single sector: Provide d that in case of financial services sector, the amount shall not exceed fifty per cent
summary: Section 47(4) mandates that retail schemes limit investments in a single sector to 25% of AUM, with an exception allowing up to 50% for the financial services sector.

content: (50%) of the AUM of the scheme: Provided further that the limit on sectoral caps shall not apply in case of a sectoral or thematic or an Index Scheme: Provided also that in case of a fund of funds scheme, the limit on sectoral cap shall not be applicable if such scheme is investing in other
summary: Section 47(4) provides that sectoral caps do not apply to sectoral, thematic, or index schemes, nor to fund of funds schemes investing in compliant underlying schemes.

content: applicable if such scheme is investing in other scheme(s) which does not have investment in a single sector in excess of 25% of their AUM, or 50% of their AUM in case of financial services sector or when such scheme(s) are sectoral or thematic or index scheme(s)
summary: Section 47(4) clarifies that fund of funds schemes are exempt from sectoral caps if the underlying schemes adhere to the 25% general or 50% financial services sector limits, or are classified as sectoral, thematic, or index schemes.

content: (5) A Retail scheme shall not invest more than twenty-five per cent
summary: Section 47(5) establishes that retail schemes are prohibited from investing more than 25% of their AUM in an associate.

content: (25%) of the AUM in its associate: Provided that this restriction shall not be applicable in case of fund of funds schemes which have made disclosure in the offer document regarding the details of the underlying scheme(s) wherein the investments are intended to be made and the nature of
summary: Section 47(5) exempts fund of funds schemes from the 25% associate investment cap, provided the offer document discloses details of underlying schemes and the nature of the FME's association.

content: are intended to be made and the nature of association, if any, that the FME has with the manager(s) of the underlying scheme(s)
summary: Section 47(5) requires disclosure of any association between the FME and the managers of underlying schemes in fund of funds arrangements to qualify for the associate investment exemption.

content: (6) The minimum size of the retail schemes shall be USD 3 Million: Provided that an open-ended scheme may commence its investment activities upon receiving at least USD 1 Million from investors and it shall receive at least USD 3 Million from investors within 12 months from the date of
summary: Section 47(6) sets a minimum retail scheme size of USD 3 million, allowing open-ended schemes to commence with USD 1 million provided the USD 3 million threshold is met within 12 months of the Authority's record of the offer document.

content: from investors within 12 months from the date of communication from the Authority, that the offer document has been taken on record: Provided further that if a FME fails to achieve the minimum investment within the specified time, it shall have a one-time option to extend the validity of the offer
summary: Section 47(6) stipulates that if an FME fails to meet the minimum investment requirement within 12 months, it may exercise a one-time option to extend the offer document validity by 6 months.

content: option to extend the validity of the offer document for a further period of 6 months by paying 50 per cent
summary: Section 47(6) specifies that a one-time 6-month extension for meeting minimum investment requirements is subject to a fee payment.

content: (50%) of the fee as applicable for filing of a fresh scheme.
summary: Section 47(6) mandates that the fee for a 6-month extension of an offer document is 50% of the fee applicable for filing a fresh scheme.

content: (1) The offer document for retail schemes shall clearly include all disclosures which are material for investors to make a decision regarding investing in such schemes
summary: Section 48(1) mandates that offer documents for retail schemes must include all material disclosures necessary for investors to make informed investment decisions.

content: (2) The disclosures in the offer document shall inter-alia include disclosures regarding the investment objective, the targeted investors, proposed size, investment style or strategy, investment methodology, proposed tenure of the scheme fees and expenses, risk management practices, KMPs of the FME
summary: Section 48(2) requires offer documents to disclose investment objectives, target investors, scheme size, strategy, methodology, tenure, fees, expenses, risk management practices, and Key Managerial Personnel (KMP) of the Fund Management Entity (FME).

content: risk management practices, KMPs of the FME and other relevant details of the FME and the scheme
summary: Section 48(2) further specifies that disclosures must include risk management practices, KMP details of the FME, and other relevant information concerning the FME and the scheme.

content: (3) Any material deviation or alteration to the fund strategy should be made with the consent of at least two-thirds (2/3 rd) of investors by value
summary: Section 48(3) stipulates that any material deviation or alteration to a fund's strategy requires the consent of at least two-thirds (2/3) of investors by value.

content: (4) The FME shall ensure that the NAV is disclosed to the investors on a daily basis in case of an open-ended scheme and on weekly basis in case of a close-ended scheme; in such manner as specified by the Authority
summary: Section 48(4) mandates that the FME disclose the Net Asset Value (NAV) daily for open-ended schemes and weekly for close-ended schemes, as specified by the Authority.

content: (5) The FME shall ensure that the portfolio under the scheme is disclosed to the investors at least on a quarterly basis within one (1) month from the end of the quarter
summary: Section 48(5) requires the FME to disclose the scheme's portfolio to investors at least quarterly, within one month from the end of each quarter.

content: (6) Any other material disclosure as considered suitable by the FME or the fiduciaries shall be informed to the investors immediately. (7) The FME and the fiduciaries shall comply with the disclosure requirements as specified above or as may be specified by the Authority.
summary: Section 48(6) and (7) require immediate disclosure of any other material information by the FME or fiduciaries and mandate compliance with all disclosure requirements set by the Authority.

content: A retail scheme shall not borrow except to meet temporary liquidity needs for the purpose of redemption: Provided that the retail scheme shall not borrow more than twenty per cent. (20%) of the AUM of the scheme and the duration of such a borrowing shall not exceed six (6) months.
summary: Section 49 restricts retail scheme borrowing to temporary liquidity needs for redemptions, capped at 20% of the Assets Under Management (AUM) with a maximum duration of six months.

content: (1) The FME and fiduciaries shall ensure compliance with the investment valuation norms as specified in the Sixth Schedule
summary: Section 50(1) requires the FME and fiduciaries to ensure compliance with investment valuation norms as prescribed in the Sixth Schedule.

content: (2) In line with the investment valuation norms, the assets of the scheme shall be valued by an independent service provider such as a fund administrator, a custodian or a credit rating agency, registered with the Authority, or a valuer registered with Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India or
summary: Section 50(2) mandates that scheme assets be valued by an independent service provider, such as a registered fund administrator, custodian, credit rating agency, or a valuer registered with the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India.

content: with Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India or such other person as may be specified by the Authority: Provided that the above requirement shall not apply in case of a fund of funds scheme that invest in scheme(s), regulated by a financial sector regulator, directly or through a manager, in IFSC
summary: Section 50 mandates registration with the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India, with an exemption for fund of funds schemes investing in schemes regulated by a financial sector regulator in the IFSC.

content: regulator, directly or through a manager, in IFSC or India or foreign jurisdiction(s), which are valued by any independent entity.
summary: Section 50 specifies that investments in IFSC, India, or foreign jurisdictions must be valued by an independent entity when managed directly or through a manager.

content: (1) The FME shall ensure that the NAV of each retail scheme is computed on a daily basis in case of an open-ended scheme and on a weekly basis in case of a close-ended scheme; in such manner as specified by the Authority
summary: Section 51(1) requires the Fund Management Entity (FME) to compute the Net Asset Value (NAV) of retail schemes daily for open-ended schemes and weekly for close-ended schemes as specified by the Authority.

content: (2) The procedure and methodology for calculating the NAV should be fully documented, and such documentation should be regularly verified and amended.
summary: Section 51(2) mandates that the procedure and methodology for NAV calculation must be fully documented, regularly verified, and amended.

content: (1) Under a retail scheme, the FME or its associate shall invest at least one per cent
summary: Section 52(1) requires the FME or its associate to invest at least one percent of the retail scheme's Assets Under Management (AUM) or USD 200,000, whichever is lower.

content: (1%) of the AUM of the retail scheme or USD 200,000, whichever is lower: Provided that the contribution by the FME or its associate shall not be mandatory in case of relocated funds /schemes established or incorporated or registered outside India to IFSC
summary: Section 52(1) provides an exemption from the mandatory one percent AUM contribution for relocated funds or schemes originally established, incorporated, or registered outside India to the IFSC.

content: Provided further that the contribution by the FME or its associate shall not be mandatory in case of a fund of funds scheme investing in scheme(s) which has similar requirements
summary: Section 52(1) further exempts fund of funds schemes from the mandatory contribution requirement if the underlying schemes already maintain similar investment requirements.

content: (2) The said contribution shall be made by the FME or its associate within forty-five (45) days and be maintained on ongoing basis: Provided that the period of forty-five (45) days may be extended subject to the satisfaction of the Authority
summary: Section 52(2) stipulates that the required contribution must be made within 45 days and maintained on an ongoing basis, subject to potential extensions granted by the Authority.

content: (3) The said contribution, if brought in by FME, may be taken into consideration for the purpose of net-worth requirements as detailed under
summary: Section 52(3) allows the FME's mandatory contribution to be counted toward meeting the applicable net-worth requirements.

content: A Registered FME may launch a special situation fund in accordance with the provisions of this Chapter.
summary: Section 53 authorizes a Registered FME to launch a special situation fund in compliance with the provisions of this Chapter.

content: For the purpose of this part of Chapter III, (1) “special situation asset” includes:- (a) stressed loan available for acquisition in terms of Clause 58 of Master Direction – Reserve Bank of India (Transfer of Loan Exposures) Directions, 2021, as amended from time to time or as part of a resolution
summary: Section 54 defines 'special situation asset' to include stressed loans available for acquisition under Clause 58 of the RBI Master Direction (Transfer of Loan Exposures) Directions, 2021.

content: from time to time or as part of a resolution plan approved under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016 or in terms of any other policy of the Reserve Bank of India or IFSCA or Government of India issued in this regard from time to time; (b) security receipts issued by an Asset Reconstruction
summary: Section 54 includes as 'special situation assets' stressed loans acquired via resolution plans under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, or policies issued by the RBI, IFSCA, or Government of India, and security receipts issued by an Asset Reconstruction Company.

content: receipts issued by an Asset Reconstruction Company registered with the Reserve Bank of India; (c) securities of investee companies: (i) whose stressed loans are available for acquisition in terms of Clause 58 of the Master Direction – Reserve Bank of India (Transfer of Loan Exposures) Directions,
summary: Section 54 defines 'special situation asset' to include securities of investee companies whose stressed loans are eligible for acquisition under Clause 58 of the RBI Master Direction (Transfer of Loan Exposures) Directions, 2021.

content: of India (Transfer of Loan Exposures) Directions, 2021, as amended from time to time, a resolution plan approved under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, or any other policy of the Reserve Bank of India or IFSCA or Government of India issued in this regard from time to time; (ii) against
summary: Section 54 classifies securities of investee companies as 'special situation assets' if they are subject to resolution plans under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, or policies of the RBI, IFSCA, or Government of India.

content: in this regard from time to time; (ii) against whose borrowings, security receipts have been issued by an Asset Reconstruction Company registered with the Reserve Bank of India; (iii) whose borrowings are subject to corporate insolvency resolution process under
summary: Section 54 includes as 'special situation assets' securities of investee companies against which an Asset Reconstruction Company has issued security receipts or whose borrowings are undergoing a corporate insolvency resolution process.
Section 55 pertains to the regulatory framework for special situation funds.

content: 55. (1) A Registered FME may launch a special situation fund through a private placement memorandum by filing the memorandum with the Authority along with the applicable fees in the manner as specified by the Authority (2) The filing of scheme documents for restricted schemes shall be under a green
summary: Section 55(1) mandates that a Registered FME must launch a special situation fund by filing a private placement memorandum with the Authority and paying the prescribed fees.

content: for restricted schemes shall be under a green channel whereby the schemes filed shall be open for subscription by investors immediately upon communication from the Authority to the FME that the placement memorandum has been taken on record: Provided that the Authority may, at any stage, offer its
summary: Section 55(2) establishes a green channel for restricted schemes, allowing immediate subscription upon the Authority confirming the placement memorandum has been taken on record.

content: that the Authority may, at any stage, offer its comments which shall suitably be incorporated by the FME in the placement memorandum
summary: Section 55(2) provides that the Authority reserves the right to offer comments at any stage, which the FME must incorporate into the placement memorandum.

content: Explanation.- For the purpose of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the communication received by the FME from the Authority that the placement memorandum has been taken on record, shall be construed as Certificate of Registration
summary: Section 55 clarifies that for the purposes of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the Authority's communication confirming the placement memorandum is taken on record serves as the Certificate of Registration.

content: (3) The validity of the placement memorandum for launch of the scheme shall be twelve (12) months from the date of communication from the Authority, that the placement memorandum has been taken on record, during which period the FME shall declare the first close of the scheme by achieving at least
summary: Under Section 55(3), the placement memorandum for a scheme is valid for twelve months from the Authority's record date, within which the FME must achieve the first close.

content: first close of the scheme by achieving at least the minimum size of corpus as specified under sub-regulation (1) of regulation 58: Provided also that if a FME fails to achieve the minimum size of corpus as specified under sub-regulation (1) of regulation 58 within the specified time, it shall have
summary: The FME must achieve the minimum corpus size specified in regulation 58(1) within the twelve-month validity period of the placement memorandum.

content: 58 within the specified time, it shall have the one-time option to extend the validity of the placement memorandum for a further period of 6 months by paying 50 per cent
summary: If the minimum corpus is not achieved, the FME may exercise a one-time option to extend the placement memorandum validity by six months upon payment of 50% of the fresh scheme filing fee.

content: (50%) of the fee as applicable for filing of a fresh scheme. (4) The FME shall ensure that any material changes in the placement memorandum are informed to the Authority immediately.
summary: Section 55(4) mandates that the FME must immediately notify the Authority of any material changes to the placement memorandum.

content: 56. (1) A special situation fund shall only be a close-ended fund. (2) The tenure of a special situation fund, which shall not be less than three (3) years, shall be disclosed in the placement memorandum
summary: Section 56(1) and (2) mandate that a special situation fund must be close-ended with a minimum tenure of three years, which must be disclosed in the placement memorandum.

content: (2) The tenure of a special situation fund may be extended up to two (2) years, subject to approval of two-thirds (2/3 rd) of the investors by value of their investment in the scheme
summary: The tenure of a special situation fund may be extended by up to two years, provided two-thirds of investors by investment value approve the extension.

content: (3) Further extension to the tenure of a special situation fund beyond the two (2) years period shall be subject to express consent of the willing investors and exit opportunity being made available to the dissenting investors
summary: Extensions beyond the initial two-year period require express consent from willing investors and must provide an exit opportunity for dissenting investors.

content: (3) A special situation fund shall be constituted in IFSC as a company or LLP or Trust or any other form as may be permitted by the Authority under the applicable laws of India.
summary: A special situation fund must be constituted in the IFSC as a company, LLP, Trust, or other form permitted by the Authority under Indian law.

content: 57. A special situation fund shall invest only in special situation assets.
summary: Section 57 restricts the investment scope of a special situation fund exclusively to special situation assets.

content: 58. (1) A special situation fund shall have the minimum corpus as may be specified by the Authority. (2) A special situation fund shall accept such eligible investors as may be specified by the Authority
summary: Section 58 mandates that special situation funds must meet minimum corpus requirements and accept only eligible investors as specified by the Authority.

content: (3) A special situation fund shall comply with such additional investment conditions as may be specified by the Authority.
summary: Section 58(3) mandates that special situation funds must adhere to additional investment conditions as specified by the Authority.

content: 59. A special situation fund shall not borrow or engage in any leveraging activities other than to meet day-to-day operational requirements.
summary: Section 59 prohibits special situation funds from borrowing or leveraging, except for meeting day-to-day operational requirements.
Section 60 provides the regulatory framework for special situation funds.

content: 60. (1) The norms regarding disclosures, valuation, computation of NAV, contribution by the FME in the scheme as applicable to a close ended restricted scheme under (2) A special situation fund shall be considered as a category under restricted schemes and accordingly a special situation fund shall
summary: Section 60(1)-(2) classifies special situation funds as a category of restricted schemes, subjecting them to norms regarding disclosures, valuation, NAV computation, and FME contributions applicable to close-ended restricted schemes.

content: and accordingly a special situation fund shall additionally comply with such requirements as may be specified by the Authority for close ended restricted schemes from time to time.
summary: Section 60 requires special situation funds to comply with all additional requirements mandated by the Authority for close-ended restricted schemes.

content: (1) Only Registered FMEs (Retail) may launch Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) by filing the draft offer document with the Authority along with the applicable fees, at least twenty-one (21) working days before its launch
summary: Section 61(1) restricts the launch of Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) to Registered FMEs (Retail), requiring the filing of a draft offer document and fees at least 21 working days before launch.

content: (2) The validity of the offer document for the launch of the ETF shall be twelve (12) months from the date of communication from the Authority to the FME that the offer document has been taken on record
summary: Section 61(2) establishes that the validity of an ETF offer document is 12 months from the date the Authority confirms it has been taken on record.

content: Explanation.- For the purpose of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the communication received by the FME from the Authority that the offer document has been taken on record, shall be construed as Certificate of Registration
summary: Section 61 Explanation clarifies that for the Income Tax Act, 1961, the Authority's confirmation that an offer document is taken on record constitutes a Certificate of Registration.

content: (3) The FME shall ensure that the comments of the Authority are duly incorporated in the offer document prior to launch of the ETF. (4) The FME shall ensure that any material changes from the information provided in the draft offer document are informed to the Authority immediately
summary: Section 61(3)-(4) mandates that FMEs incorporate Authority comments into the offer document and immediately report any material changes from the draft offer document.

content: (5) ETF shall be mandatorily listed and traded on a recognised stock exchange and shall include: (a) Equity Index based ETFs; (b) Debt Index based ETFs; (c) Commodity based ETFs; (d) Hybrid ETFs (investing in 2 or more asset class); (e) Actively Managed ETFs; (f) Any other ETFs subject to approval
summary: Section 61(5) requires mandatory listing and trading of ETFs on a recognized stock exchange, covering equity index, debt index, commodity, hybrid, actively managed, and other approved ETF types.

content: ETFs; (f) Any other ETFs subject to approval of the concerned recognised stock exchange and the Authority
summary: Section 61(f) permits the inclusion of additional ETFs subject to the approval of the relevant recognised stock exchange and the Authority.

content: (6) No offer document of an ETF shall be filed with the Authority unless it has been approved by the fiduciaries: Provided that in case of ETFs falling under clauses (e) and (f) of sub-regulation (5) above, prior approval of recognised stock exchange(s) where such ETF is intended to be listed,
summary: Section 61(6) mandates that ETF offer documents must be approved by fiduciaries before filing with the Authority, with additional prior approval required from the relevant stock exchange for ETFs under clauses (e) and (f).

content: where such ETF is intended to be listed, shall be obtained
summary: Section 61 requires that ETFs must obtain prior approval from the recognised stock exchange where the ETF is intended to be listed.

content: (7) An ETF in IFSC shall use the identifier ‘IFSC ETF’ which identifies it as an ETF listed and traded on recognised stock exchanges, and shall be used at all places including in its name, offer document, and all advertising material.
summary: Section 61(7) mandates that all ETFs in IFSC must use the identifier 'IFSC ETF' in their name, offer documents, and all advertising materials.

content: (1) A FME may launch an ETF replicating an Equity Index of IFSC or India or foreign jurisdiction
summary: Section 62(1) authorizes a Fund Management Entity (FME) to launch ETFs that replicate Equity Indices from IFSC, India, or foreign jurisdictions.

content: (2) An Equity Index based ETF that seeks to replicate a particular index shall ensure that such index complies with the following norms:- (a) The index shall have a minimum of ten (10) stocks as its constituents; (b) For a sectoral/ thematic index, no single stock shall have more than thirty-five
summary: Section 62(2) requires Equity Index-based ETFs to track indices with at least ten constituent stocks, with specific weightage limits for sectoral or thematic indices.

content: no single stock shall have more than thirty-five per cent
summary: Section 62(2)(b) stipulates that no single stock shall exceed a 35% weightage in a sectoral or thematic index used by an ETF.

content: (35%) weightage in the index; and (c) For other than sectoral/ thematic indices, no single stock shall have more than twenty-five per cent. (25%) weightage in the index. (3) Equity Index based ETF shall replicate the underlying index to the extent of at least ninety-five per cent
summary: Section 62(2)(c) and (3) limit single-stock weightage to 25% for non-sectoral/thematic indices and require Equity Index-based ETFs to maintain at least 95% replication of the underlying index.

content: (95%) of total assets.
summary: Section 62(3) mandates that Equity Index-based ETFs must maintain a minimum of 95% of their total assets in replication of the underlying index.

content: (1) The FME may launch an ETF replicating a Debt Index of IFSC or India or foreign jurisdiction
summary: Section 63(1) authorizes an FME to launch ETFs that replicate Debt Indices from IFSC, India, or foreign jurisdictions.

content: (2) A debt index-based fund that seeks to replicate a particular index shall ensure that such index complies with the following norms:- (a) The index shall have a minimum five (5) issuers as its constituents; (b) No single issuer shall have more than twenty-five per cent
summary: Section 63(2) mandates that debt index-based funds replicating an index must ensure the index contains at least five issuers, with no single issuer exceeding a 25% weightage.

content: (25%) weightage in the index; and (c) The rating of the constituents of the index shall be investment grade: Provided that the conditions specified in clauses (a) and (b) shall not apply to index-based funds investing in Government securities
summary: Section 63(2) requires index constituents to maintain investment-grade ratings, though these diversification and issuer limits do not apply to funds investing in Government securities.

content: (3) Debt index-based ETF shall replicate the underlying index to the extent of at least ninety per cent
summary: Section 63(3) stipulates that debt index-based ETFs must replicate their underlying index using at least 90% of their total assets.

content: (90 %) of total assets: Provided that if the ETF is not able to replicate the index due to non-availability of issuances of the issuer forming part of the index, the FME may consider such deviations that would lead to least possible tracking error and shall be in line with the disclosures in the
summary: Under Section 63(3), if an ETF cannot replicate an index due to issuer non-availability, the FME may permit deviations to minimize tracking error, provided they align with offer document disclosures.

content: and shall be in line with the disclosures in the offer document.
summary: Section 63(3) requires that any permitted deviations in index replication for debt ETFs must strictly adhere to the disclosures provided in the scheme's offer document.

content: (1) Commodity based ETFs shall invest at least ninety per cent. (90%) in the specified commodity or commodity related security / instrument as specified by the Authority
summary: Section 64(1) mandates that commodity-based ETFs invest a minimum of 90% of their assets in specified commodities or commodity-related instruments as defined by the Authority.

content: (2) For launching a commodity based ETF, the FME shall ensure that a KMP with at least than five (5) years of experience in dealing with commodities, is designated as a Fund Manager.
summary: Section 64(2) requires that a commodity-based ETF must designate a Key Management Personnel (KMP) with at least five years of commodity-dealing experience as the Fund Manager.

content: (1) In case of a Gold ETF, at least ninety per cent
summary: Section 65(1) requires Gold ETFs to invest at least 90% of their Assets Under Management (AUM) in gold, bullion instruments, or gold-underlying Exchange Traded Commodity Derivatives (ETCD).

content: (90%) of the AUM shall be invested in Gold or bullion instruments such as Bullion Depository Receipts with underlying Gold, and Exchange Traded Commodity Derivatives (ETCD) with gold as the underlying: Provided that the exposure to ETCDs having gold as the underlying shall not exceed ten per cent
summary: Section 65(1) limits exposure to ETCDs with gold as the underlying to a maximum of 10% of the scheme's AUM.

content: (10%) of AUM of the scheme: Provided further that the limit of ten per cent. (10%) shall not be applicable to Gold ETFs where the intention is to take delivery of the physical Gold and not to rollover its position to next contract cycle
summary: Section 65(1) provides that the 10% ETCD exposure limit does not apply to Gold ETFs intended for physical gold delivery rather than rolling over positions to the next contract cycle.

content: (2) Gold ETFs shall be benchmarked against the price of spot Gold at the recognised stock exchange or any other benchmark price as may be specified by the Authority and FMEs shall endeavor to have tracking error as low as possible
summary: Section 65(2) mandates that Gold ETFs be benchmarked against spot Gold prices at a recognised stock exchange or Authority-specified benchmarks, requiring FMEs to minimize tracking error.

content: (3) The FMEs shall ensure that for investment in physical Gold, the Gold should be supplied by a refiner certified for complying with OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Supply Chains of Minerals from Conflict-Affected and High-Risk Areas
summary: Section 65(3) requires FMEs to source physical Gold from refiners certified under the OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Supply Chains of Minerals from Conflict-Affected and High-Risk Areas.

content: (4) Physical Gold shall be stored with a vault registered with the Authority
summary: Section 65(4) mandates that all physical Gold underlying Gold ETFs must be stored with a vault registered with the Authority.

content: (5) Physical verification of gold underlying the Gold ETF units shall be carried out by an independent agency capable of undertaking such activities and report to the FME and fiduciaries on half yearly basis and not later than two months from the end of the half year.
summary: Section 65(5) requires independent physical verification of Gold ETF underlying assets on a half-yearly basis, with reports submitted to FMEs and fiduciaries within two months of the period end.

content: (1) In case of a Silver ETF, at least ninety per cent
summary: Section 66(1) establishes that Silver ETFs must invest at least 90% of their AUM in physical Silver, bullion instruments, or Silver-based Exchange Traded Commodity Derivatives (ETCDs).

content: (90%) of the AUM should be invested in Silver or bullion instruments such as Bullion Depository Receipts with underlying Silver and Exchange Traded Commodity Derivatives (ETCD) with silver as the underlying: Provided that the exposure to ETCDs having silver as the underlying shall not exceed ten
summary: Section 66(1) limits exposure to Silver-based ETCDs to a maximum of 10% of the scheme's AUM.

content: silver as the underlying shall not exceed ten per cent
summary: Section 66(1) specifies that the 10% AUM limit on Silver ETCDs applies to the scheme's total assets.

content: (10%) of AUM of the scheme: Provided further the limit of ten per cent. (10%) shall not be applicable to Silver ETFs where the intention is to take delivery of the physical silver and not to rollover its position to next contract cycle
summary: Section 66(1) provides an exemption to the 10% ETCD exposure limit for Silver ETFs intending to take physical delivery of silver rather than rolling over positions to the next contract cycle.

content: (2) Silver ETFs shall be benchmarked against the price of spot Silver at a recognised stock exchange or any other benchmark price as may be specified by the Authority and the FMEs shall endeavor to have tracking error as low as possible
summary: Section 66(2) mandates that Silver ETFs be benchmarked against spot Silver prices at a recognised stock exchange or Authority-specified benchmarks, requiring FMEs to minimize tracking error.

content: (3) The FMEs shall ensure that for investment in physical Silver, the silver should have supplied by a refiner certified for complying with OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Supply Chains of Minerals from Conflict-Affected and High-Risk Areas
summary: Section 66(3) requires FMEs to source physical Silver from refiners certified under the OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Supply Chains of Minerals from Conflict-Affected and High-Risk Areas.

content: (4) Physical Silver shall be stored with a vault registered with the Authority
summary: Section 66(4) mandates that all physical silver underlying Silver ETF units must be stored in a vault registered with the Authority.

content: (5) Physical verification of silver underlying the Silver ETF units shall be carried out by an independent agency capable of undertaking such activities and report to the FME and fiduciaries on half yearly basis and not later than two months from the end of the half year.
summary: Section 66(5) requires an independent agency to conduct half-yearly physical verification of silver underlying Silver ETF units, with reports submitted to the FME and fiduciaries within two months of the period's end.

content: (1) Actively managed ETFs are such ETFs in which the FME has discretion over the composition of portfolio, in accordance with the stated investment objectives and policies
summary: Section 67(1) defines actively managed ETFs as those where the FME exercises discretion over portfolio composition based on stated investment objectives and policies.

content: (2) An actively managed ETF shall disclose in its offer document and in all advertising material that it is an actively managed ETF, and shall disclose how it will meet the stated investment objectives including, where applicable, its intention to outperform an index
summary: Section 67(2) mandates that actively managed ETFs must disclose their status and strategy for meeting investment objectives, including any intent to outperform an index, in all offer documents and advertising.

content: (3) Draft offer document for Actively Managed ETFs shall be filed with the recognised stock exchange(s) where such ETF is intended to be listed and also with the Authority.
summary: Section 67(3) requires the draft offer document for actively managed ETFs to be filed with both the Authority and the recognized stock exchange(s) where the ETF will be listed.

content: (1) A FME shall appoint a market maker who shall be responsible for ensuring liquidity in the trading of ETF by way of providing two-way quotes. (2) Market Makers shall be permitted to create units and seek redemptions directly from the FME
summary: Section 68(1)-(2) requires FMEs to appoint market makers to provide two-way quotes for ETF liquidity, granting these market makers the authority to create and redeem units directly with the FME.

content: (3) Recognised Stock Exchange(s) may provide a simplified framework for authorisation of intermediaries registered with the Authority as market makers. (4) Recognised Stock Exchange(s) shall also provide detailed rules for market makers viz
summary: Section 68(3)-(4) authorizes recognized stock exchanges to establish simplified frameworks for market maker authorization and mandates the creation of detailed rules governing their operations.

content: maximum spread, minimum quantity, if any, incentives, margining, net-settlement etc.
summary: Section 68(4) specifies that market maker rules must cover operational parameters including maximum spreads, minimum quantities, incentives, margining, and net-settlement procedures.

content: (1) A FME shall compute the NAV of each ETF on a daily basis and publish the same on its website and inform the same to the recognised stock exchange where it is listed for disclosure on its website
summary: Section 69(1) requires FMEs to compute the Net Asset Value (NAV) of each ETF daily and publish it on their website and the relevant stock exchange's website.

content: (2) The procedure and methodology for calculating the NAV should be fully documented, and such documentation should be regularly verified and amended, if required.
summary: Section 69(2) mandates that the methodology and procedures for NAV calculation must be fully documented, regularly verified, and amended as necessary.

content: Investors other than market makers may also directly approach the FME for redemption of ETFs, and no exit load shall be charged if: (a) Traded price (based on closing price) of the ETF units is at a discount of more than five per cent
summary: Under Section 70, investors other than market makers may approach the FME for ETF redemption without exit load if the traded price is at a discount of more than 5% of NAV for 30 consecutive trading days.

content: (5%) of NAV for continuous thirty (30) trading days; or (b) No quotes are available on the recognised stock exchange for five (5) consecutive trading days; or (c) Total bid size on the recognised stock exchange is less than higher of one per cent
summary: Section 70 permits exit-load-free redemption if no quotes are available on the stock exchange for five consecutive trading days or if the total bid size falls below specified thresholds.

content: (1%) of the total units valued at NAV in ETF or USD 2,500 in value, averaged over a period of seven (7) consecutive trading days.
summary: Section 70 defines the bid size threshold for exit-load-free redemption as the higher of 1% of total units valued at NAV or USD 2,500, averaged over seven consecutive trading days.

content: (1) The offer document for ETFs shall clearly include all disclosures which are material for investors to make an informed decision regarding investing in such ETFs
summary: Section 71(1) mandates that ETF offer documents must include all material disclosures necessary for investors to make informed investment decisions.

content: (2) The disclosures in the offer document shall inter-alia include investment objective, the targeted investors, investment style or strategy, investment methodology, proposed fees and expenses, risk management practices, KMPs of the FME and other relevant details of the FME and the ETF
summary: Section 71(2) requires offer documents to disclose investment objectives, strategies, methodologies, fees, expenses, risk management practices, and details regarding the FME and its KMPs.

content: (3) Any material deviation or alteration to the fund strategy should be made with the consent of at least two-thirds (2/3 rd) of investors by value
summary: Section 71(3) stipulates that any material deviation or alteration to an ETF's fund strategy requires the consent of at least two-thirds of investors by value.

content: (4) The FME shall ensure that the NAV is disclosed to the investors on a daily basis and the manner of such disclosure shall be detailed in the offer document
summary: Section 71(4) requires the FME to disclose the ETF's NAV to investors on a daily basis, with the specific disclosure methodology defined in the offer document.

content: (5) The FME shall ensure that the portfolio under the ETF is disclosed to the investors and the manner of such disclosure shall be detailed in the offer document. (6) Any other material disclosure as considered suitable by the FME or the fiduciaries shall be informed to the investors immediately
summary: Section 71(5) and (6) mandate the disclosure of the ETF portfolio and require the immediate communication of any other material information deemed suitable by the FME or fiduciaries.

content: (7) The FME and the fiduciaries shall ensure compliance with the disclosure requirements as specified above or as may be specified by the Authority.
summary: Section 71(7) mandates that the FME and fiduciaries ensure compliance with all specified disclosure requirements and any additional requirements set by the Authority.

summary: Section 72(1) mandates that a Fund Management Entity (FME) with Assets Under Management (AUM) exceeding USD 3 billion must establish governance policies for material sustainability-related risks and opportunities.

summary: Section 73(1) authorizes a Registered FME to provide Portfolio Management Services to clients in compliance with these regulations.
