content: (5) The Authority, on receipt of all information and on being satisfied, may grant Certificate of Registration as an Investment Trust, subject to the applicant paying the applicable fee.
summary: Under Section 84(5), the Authority may grant a Certificate of Registration as an Investment Trust upon receipt of all required information and payment of the applicable fee.

content: The following are the eligibility conditions for the Investment trust: (a) The trust shall be created under the laws of India (IFSC or outside IFSC) or foreign jurisdiction; (b) The trust deed has its main objective as undertaking activity of Investment Trust (REIT or InvIT, as the case may be) and
summary: Section 85 mandates that an Investment Trust must be created under Indian or foreign jurisdiction laws and possess a trust deed with the primary objective of operating as a REIT or InvIT.

content: Trust (REIT or InvIT, as the case may be) and includes responsibilities of the Trustee in accordance with the requirements specified in these regulations; (c) persons have been designated as sponsor(s), investment manager and trustee and all such persons are separate entities.
summary: Section 85 requires the trust deed to define Trustee responsibilities and mandates that the sponsor, investment manager, and trustee must be separate, distinct entities.

content: The following are the eligibility conditions for a sponsor of an Investment Trust: (a) Each sponsor shall hold or propose to hold not less than five per cent. (5%) of the number of units of the Investment Trust on post-initial offer basis: Provided that in case the holding goes below five per cent
summary: Section 86 requires each sponsor to hold at least 5% of Investment Trust units post-initial offer, with a one-year grace period to restore this holding if it falls below the threshold.

content: (5%), the sponsor shall comply with the requirement within a time period of one year from the date of such decline; (b) Each sponsor shall maintain net worth not less than USD 15 million if it is a body corporate or a company, or net tangible assets of value not less than USD 15 million in case it
summary: Section 86 mandates that corporate sponsors maintain a net worth of at least USD 15 million, or USD 15 million in net tangible assets for limited liability partnerships.

content: of value not less than USD 15 million in case it is a limited liability partnership: Provided that in case of REIT, each sponsor shall maintain a net worth of not less than USD 3 million and the sponsor(s), on a collective basis, shall maintain a net worth of not less than USD 15 million; (c) The
summary: For REITs under Section 86, individual sponsors must maintain a minimum net worth of USD 3 million, while the collective sponsor group must maintain a minimum net worth of USD 15 million.

content: worth of not less than USD 15 million; (c) The sponsor(s) or its associate(s) shall have a sound track record in development of real estate or infrastructure or fund management in the infrastructure / real estate sector
summary: Section 86 requires sponsors or their associates to demonstrate a sound track record in real estate or infrastructure development or fund management within those sectors.

content: Explanation.- For the purpose of this sub-regulation, ‘sound track record’ means relevant experience of at least five (5) years, and where the sponsor is a developer, at least two projects of the sponsor have been completed.
summary: Under Section 86, a 'sound track record' is defined as at least five years of relevant experience, with developers required to have completed at least two projects.

content: (1) In case of private placement, any Registered FME shall be eligible to be appointed as Investment Manager. (2) In case of a public issues, only a Registered FME (Retail) shall be eligible to be appointed as an Investment Manager.
summary: Section 87 stipulates that any Registered FME may serve as an Investment Manager for private placements, while only a Registered FME (Retail) is eligible for public issues.

content: The following are the eligibility conditions for a trustee of an Investment Trust: (a) The entity shall be authorised / registered as a trustee with the Authority or any other securities market regulator and shall not be an associate of the sponsor(s) or investment manager; (b) The trustee has
summary: Section 88 requires a trustee to be registered with the Authority or a securities market regulator and prohibits the trustee from being an associate of the sponsor or investment manager.

content: or investment manager; (b) The trustee has necessary wherewithal with respect to infrastructure, personnel, etc
summary: Section 88 requires the trustee of an Investment Trust to possess the necessary infrastructure and personnel to support the investment manager.

content: to the satisfaction of the Authority and the recognised stock exchange(s).
summary: Section 88 mandates that the trustee's operational capabilities must be demonstrated to the satisfaction of the Authority and the relevant recognised stock exchange(s).

content: (1) The registration shall be granted only if the Investment Trust and parties to the Investment Trust are fit and proper persons as specified under these regulations
summary: Section 89(1) stipulates that registration is contingent upon the Investment Trust and its associated parties meeting the 'fit and proper' person criteria defined in the regulations.

content: (2) The Parties to an Investment Trust shall perform their roles and discharge their responsibilities as specified in the Fourth Schedule .
summary: Section 89(2) mandates that all parties involved in an Investment Trust must execute their roles and responsibilities as prescribed in the Fourth Schedule.

content: The units of the Investment Trust shall only be in a freely convertible foreign currency.
summary: Section 90 requires that all units issued by an Investment Trust must be denominated in a freely convertible foreign currency.

content: (1) No initial offer of units by an Investment Trust shall be made unless,– (a) the Investment Trust is registered with the Authority; (b) the value of assets of Investment Trust is not less than USD 75 million; and (c) the offer size is not less than USD 35 million
summary: Section 91(1) prohibits an initial offer of units unless the Investment Trust is registered with the Authority, holds assets of at least USD 75 million, and has an offer size of at least USD 35 million.

content: (2) The minimum offer and allotment of units, to public through an offer document or placement memorandum in respect of Investment Trust, proposed to be listed on a recognised stock exchange shall be as per the below table: Post Issue Capital Minimum no./ value of units to be offered Less than USD
summary: Section 91(2) establishes the minimum offer and allotment requirements for units of an Investment Trust intended for listing on a recognised stock exchange based on post-issue capital.

content: no./ value of units to be offered Less than USD 240 million At least twenty-five per cent
summary: Section 91(2) specifies that for post-issue capital of less than USD 240 million, at least 25% of the total outstanding units must be offered to the public.

content: (25%) of the total outstanding units of the Investment Trust USD 240 million or more but less than USD 600 million At least USD 60 million Equal to or more than USD 600 million At least ten per cent
summary: Section 91(2) mandates a minimum public offer of USD 60 million for capital between USD 240 million and USD 600 million, and at least 10% of total outstanding units for capital equal to or exceeding USD 600 million.

content: (10%) of the total outstanding units of the Investment Trust Provided that the units offered to sponsor or the investment manager or the project manager or their related parties or their associates shall not be counted towards units offered to the public.
summary: Section 91(2) clarifies that units offered to the sponsor, investment manager, project manager, or their associates/related parties are excluded from the calculation of units offered to the public.

content: The fund raising by an Investment Trust by way of private placement whose units are proposed to be listed on the recognised stock exchange(s) shall be subject to compliance with the following: (a) The Investment Trust shall obtain in-principle approval from the recognised stock exchange(s):
summary: Section 92 mandates that Investment Trusts raising funds via private placement for listing on a recognized stock exchange must obtain in-principle approval from said exchange.

content: approval from the recognised stock exchange(s): Provided that the in-principle approval shall be valid for a period not greater than six (6) months from the date of its receipt from the recognised stock exchange
summary: Under Section 92, in-principle approval granted by a recognized stock exchange for an Investment Trust is valid for a maximum period of six months from the date of receipt.

content: (b) The Investment Trust, through the Investment Manager or Investment Banker, shall file a placement memorandum with the Authority along with the applicable fee at least five (5) working days prior to opening of the issue: Provided that such opening of the issue shall not be at a date later than
summary: Section 92 requires the Investment Trust, via its Investment Manager or Banker, to file a placement memorandum and pay applicable fees to the Authority at least five working days before the issue opens.

content: of the issue shall not be at a date later than six (6) months from the receipt of in-principle approval for listing, from recognised stock exchange(s)
summary: Per Section 92, the opening of a private placement issue for a listed Investment Trust must occur within six months of receiving in-principle approval from the recognized stock exchange.

content: (c) The Investment Trust shall raise funds from accredited investor or from investors investing at least USD 150,000: Provided that if a privately placed Investment Trust invests or proposes to invest less than eighty per cent of the value of the assets of Investment Trust in completed and revenue
summary: Section 92 stipulates that Investment Trusts must raise funds from accredited investors or those investing at least USD 150,000, with higher thresholds for trusts with lower allocations in revenue-generating assets.

content: of Investment Trust in completed and revenue generating assets, the minimum investment from each investor shall be USD 1 million
summary: Under Section 92, if an Investment Trust invests less than 80% of its asset value in completed and revenue-generating assets, the minimum investment requirement per investor increases to USD 1 million.

content: (d) The Investment Trust shall raise funds from at least two (2) and not more than one thousand (1000) investors
summary: Section 92 mandates that an Investment Trust must raise funds from a minimum of two and a maximum of one thousand investors.

content: (e) The Investment Trust, through the Investment Manager, shall file the final placement memorandum with the Authority within a period of ten (10) working days from the date of listing of the units.
summary: Section 92 requires the Investment Manager to file the final placement memorandum with the Authority within ten working days following the listing of the Investment Trust's units.

content: The fund raising by an Investment Trust by way of private placement, whose units are not proposed to be listed on the recognised stock exchange(s), shall be subject to compliance with the following: (a) The Investment Trust shall file a draft placement memorandum with the Authority in the manner as
summary: Section 93 governs private placement fund raising for Investment Trusts not intended for listing, requiring the filing of a draft placement memorandum with the Authority.

content: memorandum with the Authority in the manner as specified by the Authority, along with the applicable fee, at least five (5) working days prior to opening of the issue: Provided that such opening of the issue shall not be at a date later than three (3) months from the date of communication from the
summary: Under Section 93, the draft placement memorandum and applicable fees must be filed with the Authority at least five working days before the issue opens, which must occur within three months of the Authority's communication.

content: months from the date of communication from the Authority that the placement memorandum has been taken on record
summary: Section 93 establishes a timeline for actions based on the date of communication from the Authority regarding the placement memorandum being taken on record.

content: (b) The Investment Trust shall raise funds from investors investing at least USD 250,000 or accredited investors. (c) The Investment Trust shall raise funds from not more than fifty (50) investors
summary: Under Section 93, an Investment Trust must restrict fundraising to accredited investors or those investing at least USD 250,000, with a maximum limit of fifty (50) investors.

content: (d) The Investment Trust shall file the final placement memorandum with the Authority within a period of ten (10) working days from the date of allotment of the units to the investors.
summary: Section 93 mandates that the Investment Trust file the final placement memorandum with the Authority within ten (10) working days following the allotment of units.

content: The fund raising by an Investment Trust by way of public issue, shall be subject to compliance with the following: (a) The Investment Trust shall raise funds by way of initial public offer and any subsequent issue of units may be by way of follow-on offer, preferential allotment, qualified
summary: Section 94 governs public issue fundraising for Investment Trusts, permitting mechanisms including initial public offers, follow-on offers, and preferential allotments.

content: offer, preferential allotment, qualified institutional placement, rights issue, bonus issue, offer for sale or any other mechanism and in such manner as may be specified by the Authority
summary: Section 94 authorizes Investment Trusts to utilize qualified institutional placements, rights issues, bonus issues, or offers for sale as specified by the Authority.

content: (b) The Investment Trust, through the Investment Manager or an investment banker appointed by it, shall file a draft offer document along with the applicable fee, not less than thirty (30) working days before filing the offer document with the recognised stock exchange and the Authority
summary: Section 94 requires the Investment Manager or appointed investment banker to file a draft offer document with the Authority and stock exchange at least thirty (30) working days prior to the final filing.

content: (c) The draft offer document filed with the Authority shall be made public for comments, if any, by hosting it on the websites of the Authority, recognised stock exchange(s) and Investment Manager, investment bankers associated with the issue, for a period of not less than fourteen (14) days
summary: Section 94 mandates that draft offer documents be hosted on the websites of the Authority, stock exchanges, and relevant managers for public comment for at least fourteen (14) days.

content: (d) The draft offer document and/ or offer document shall be accompanied by a due diligence certificate signed by the investment manager or investment banker, as the case may be
summary: Section 94 requires that all draft and final offer documents be accompanied by a due diligence certificate signed by the investment manager or investment banker.

content: (e) The Authority may communicate its observations to the investment manager or investment banker within thirty (30) working days from the filing of the offer document or twenty- one (21) working days from the date of receipt of satisfactory reply from the investment manager or investment banker on
summary: Section 94 outlines the Authority's timeline for communicating observations on offer documents, set at thirty (30) working days from filing or twenty-one (21) working days from receipt of satisfactory clarifications.

content: the investment manager or investment banker on the queries or clarification, if any, sought by the Authority, whichever is later
summary: Section 94 specifies that the Authority's observation period is determined by the later of the initial filing date or the date of receipt of responses to queries or clarifications.

content: (f) The investment manager or investment banker shall ensure that all comments received from the Authority on the draft offer document are suitably incorporated prior to the filing of the offer document with the recognised stock exchanges
summary: Under Section 94(f), the investment manager or banker must incorporate all Authority comments into the draft offer document before filing with recognized stock exchanges.

content: (g) The offer document shall be filed with the recognised stock exchange(s) and the Authority not less than five (5) working days before opening of the offer
summary: Section 94(g) mandates that the offer document be filed with the Authority and recognized stock exchanges at least five working days prior to the offer opening.

content: (h) The initial offer or follow-on offer or rights issue shall be made by the Investment Trust within a period of not more than one (1) year from the date of issuance of observations by the Authority: Provided that if the initial offer or follow-on offer or rights issue is not made within the
summary: Section 94(h) requires an Investment Trust to launch an initial, follow-on, or rights issue within one year of the Authority's observations.

content: offer or rights issue is not made within the specified time period, a fresh draft offer document shall be filed
summary: Per Section 94(h), failure to launch an offer within the one-year validity period of the Authority's observations necessitates the filing of a fresh draft offer document.

content: (i) The Investment Trust may invite for subscriptions and allot units to investors, subject to the applicable investment guidelines and permissions as may be applicable in that jurisdiction
summary: Section 94(i) authorizes an Investment Trust to invite subscriptions and allot units to investors, provided it complies with applicable jurisdictional investment guidelines and permissions.

content: (j) The initial offer and follow-on offer shall not be open for subscription for a period more than thirty (30) days
summary: Section 94(j) restricts the subscription period for initial and follow-on offers to a maximum of thirty days.

content: (k) In case of over-subscriptions, the Investment Trust shall allot units to the applicants on a proportionate basis rounded off to the nearest integer, subject to minimum subscription amount per subscriber
summary: Section 94(k) stipulates that over-subscriptions must be allotted on a proportionate basis, rounded to the nearest integer, while adhering to minimum subscription requirements.

content: (l) The Investment Trust shall allot units or refund application money, as the case may be, within twelve (12) working days from the date of closing of the issue. (m) The Investment Trust shall issue units only in dematerialized form to all the applicants
summary: Section 94(l) and (m) require the Investment Trust to allot units or refund money within twelve working days of issue closure and mandate that all units be issued in dematerialized form.

content: (n) The price of Investment Trust units issued by way of public issue shall be determined through the book building process or in accordance with the circulars or guidelines issued by the Authority or the recognised stock exchange(s). (o) The Investment Trust shall refund money, - i
summary: Section 94(n) requires unit pricing for public issues to be determined via book building or Authority-prescribed guidelines, while 94(o) initiates refund requirements.

content: (o) The Investment Trust shall refund money, - i. to all applicants in case it fails to collect subscription amount of exceeding ninety per cent. (90%) of the fresh issue size as specified in the offer document; ii
summary: Section 94(o)(i) mandates that the Investment Trust refund all application money if the subscription amount fails to exceed ninety percent of the fresh issue size specified in the offer document.

content: to applicants to the extent of oversubscription in case the money received is in excess of the extent of over-subscription as specified in the offer document: Provided that right to retain such over subscription cannot exceed twenty-five per cent
summary: Section 94 limits the retention of oversubscription funds to a maximum of twenty-five percent (25%) of the total issue size.

content: (25%) of the issue size: Provided further that the offer document shall contain adequate disclosures towards the utilisation of such oversubscription proceeds, if any, and such proceeds retained on account of oversubscription shall not be utilised towards general purposes
summary: Section 94 mandates that offer documents disclose the utilization of oversubscription proceeds and prohibits the use of such retained funds for general corporate purposes.

content: (p) Units may be offered for sale to public if such units have been held by the existing unitholders for a period of at least one (1) year prior to the filing of draft offer document with the Authority: Provided that the holding period for the equity shares, compulsorily convertible securities,
summary: Section 94(p) permits the public sale of units provided existing unitholders have held them for at least one year prior to filing the draft offer document.

content: shares, compulsorily convertible securities, from the date such securities are fully paid-up, or partnership interest in the holdco and/or SPV against which such units have been received shall be considered for the purpose of calculation of one (1) year period referred in this regulation: Provided
summary: Section 94(p) specifies that the one-year holding period calculation includes the time equity shares, compulsorily convertible securities, or partnership interests in the holdco or SPV were held.

content: year period referred in this regulation: Provided further that the compulsorily convertible securities, whose holding period has been included for the purpose of calculation for offer for sale, shall be converted to equity shares of the holdco or SPV, prior to filing of offer document
summary: Section 94(p) requires that any compulsorily convertible securities included in the one-year holding period calculation must be converted into equity shares of the holdco or SPV before filing the offer document.

content: (q) The amount for general purposes, as mentioned in objects of the issue in the draft offer document filed with the Authority, shall not exceed ten per cent. (10%) of the amount raised by the Investment Trust by issuance of units.
summary: Section 94(q) restricts the amount allocated for general purposes in the draft offer document to a maximum of ten percent (10%) of the total funds raised by the Investment Trust.

content: (1) The offer document or the placement memorandum of an Investment Trust shall contain material, true, correct and adequate disclosures to enable the investors to make an informed investment decision
summary: Section 95(1) mandates that offer documents or placement memoranda for Investment Trusts must provide material, true, correct, and adequate disclosures to facilitate informed investor decisions.

content: (2) The offer document or placement memorandum shall include all information as required by the recognised stock exchange(s) and in such format as specified by the recognised stock exchange(s), including: (a) Introduction; (b) Details of sponsor(s), Investment Manager, Project Manager, Trustee and
summary: Section 95(2) requires offer documents to include information mandated by recognized stock exchanges, including details on the sponsor, Investment Manager, Project Manager, and Trustee.

content: Investment Manager, Project Manager, Trustee and other relevant parties; (c) Brief background of the Investment Trust; (d) Description of the assets under the Investment Trust; (e) Business Details and Strategy; (f) Leverage; (g) Conflict of Interest and Related party transactions; (h) Valuation;
summary: Section 95(2) specifies that offer documents must disclose the Investment Trust's background, asset descriptions, business strategy, leverage, conflict of interest, related party transactions, and valuation.

content: and Related party transactions; (h) Valuation; (i) Financials; (j) Rights of Unit Holders; (k) Title disclosures, litigations and regulatory actions; (l) Risk Factors; (m) Taxation
summary: Section 95(2) requires the inclusion of financial statements, unit holder rights, title disclosures, litigation, regulatory actions, risk factors, and taxation details in the offer document.

content: (3) The following documents shall be submitted to the Authority: (a) Valuation report along with the offer document/ placement memorandum; (b) Project implementation/management agreement, along with draft offer document or the placement memorandum; (c) Due diligence certificate along with the draft
summary: Section 95(3) mandates the submission of a valuation report, project implementation/management agreement, and due diligence certificate alongside the draft offer document or placement memorandum to the Authority.

content: Due diligence certificate along with the draft offer document and offer document/ placement memorandum; and (d) In principle approval from the recognised stock exchange(s)
summary: Section 95(3) requires the inclusion of a due diligence certificate with the draft offer document and placement memorandum, as well as in-principle approval from recognized stock exchanges.

content: (4) The placement memorandum of an Investment Trust whose units are not proposed to be listed on a recognised stock exchange shall contain all information, to the extent applicable, as specified for an Investment Trust whose units are proposed to be listed on a recognised stock exchange.
summary: Section 95(4) stipulates that the placement memorandum for an unlisted Investment Trust must contain all applicable information required for an Investment Trust whose units are proposed to be listed.

content: (1) The Investment Trust listed on a recognised stock exchange shall comply with the continuous obligations and disclosure requirements specified by the recognised stock exchange(s)
summary: Section 96(1) mandates that Investment Trusts listed on a recognized stock exchange must adhere to all continuous obligations and disclosure requirements prescribed by those exchanges.

content: (2) The following disclosure requirements shall be applicable on an Investment Trust whose units are not listed on a recognised stock exchange: (a) The investment manager of the Investment Trust shall submit annual report, half-yearly report and valuation report to the trustee and unit holders of
summary: Section 96(2)(a) requires the investment manager of an unlisted Investment Trust to provide annual, half-yearly, and valuation reports to the trustee and unit holders via electronic or physical means.

content: report to the trustee and unit holders of the Investment Trust, either electronically or through physical copies; (b) The annual and half yearly reports shall contain disclosures as specified for a privately placed Investment Trusts listed on a recognised stock exchange; (c) The investment manager
summary: Section 96(2)(b) mandates that annual and half-yearly reports for unlisted Investment Trusts must include disclosures equivalent to those required for privately placed Investment Trusts listed on a stock exchange.

content: stock exchange; (c) The investment manager shall disclose to the trustee and unit holders any information having bearing on the operation or performance of the Investment Trust which includes but is not restricted to the following:– i
summary: Section 96(2)(c) requires the investment manager to disclose to the trustee and unit holders any information significantly impacting the operation or performance of the Investment Trust.

content: acquisition or disposal of any infrastructure projects or property, directly or through holdco or SPV, value of which exceeds five per cent. (5%) of value of the assets of Investment Trust; ii. additional issue of units by the Investment Trust; iii
summary: Section 96(2)(c)(i-ii) mandates disclosure of infrastructure project or property acquisitions/disposals exceeding 5% of asset value and any additional issuance of units by the Investment Trust.

content: details of any credit rating obtained by the Investment Trust and any change in such rating; iv. any issue which requires approval of the unit holders; v. any legal proceedings which may have significant bearing on the functioning of the Investment Trust; vi
summary: Section 96(2)(c)(iii-vi) requires disclosure of credit ratings and changes, matters requiring unit holder approval, significant legal proceedings, and notices or results of unit holder meetings.

content: notices and results of meetings of unit holders, vii. any instance of non-compliance with these regulations and the circulars or guidelines issued thereunder including any breach of limits; and viii
summary: Section 96(2)(c)(vii-viii) mandates disclosure of any instances of non-compliance with regulations, circulars, or guidelines, including breaches of established limits.

content: any material issue that in the opinion of the investment manager or trustee needs to be disclosed to the unit holders.
summary: Section 96 mandates the disclosure of any material issue to unit holders if deemed necessary by the investment manager or trustee.

content: (1) The Investment Trust may invest in infrastructure projects or properties, as the case may be, through SPVs, subject to the following:- (a) no other shareholder or partner of the SPV shall exercise any rights that prevents the Investment Trust from complying with the regulatory requirements
summary: Section 97(1)(a) permits Investment Trusts to invest in infrastructure or properties via SPVs, provided no other shareholder or partner restricts the Trust's compliance with regulatory requirements.

content: from complying with the regulatory requirements specified by Authority and recognised stock exchange(s), and an agreement has been entered into with such shareholders or partners to that effect prior to investment in the SPV: Provided that the shareholders’ agreement or partnership agreement shall
summary: Section 97 requires an agreement with SPV shareholders or partners prior to investment to ensure compliance with Authority and stock exchange regulatory requirements.

content: agreement or partnership agreement shall provide for an appropriate mechanism for resolution of disputes between the Investment Trust and the other shareholders or partners in the SPV: Provided further that the provisions specified by Authority and recognised stock exchange(s) shall prevail in case
summary: Section 97 mandates that shareholder or partnership agreements include dispute resolution mechanisms between the Investment Trust and other SPV stakeholders.

content: stock exchange(s) shall prevail in case of inconsistencies between such agreement(s) and the obligations cast upon an Investment Trust by the Authority and recognised stock exchange(s)
summary: Section 97 stipulates that regulatory obligations imposed by the Authority and stock exchanges prevail over any inconsistent provisions in shareholder or partnership agreements.

content: (b) in case the SPV is a company or LLP, the investment manager shall in consultation with the trustee appoint majority of the board of directors or governing board of such SPVs as applicable: Provided that in case of a REIT, the investment manager shall in consultation with the trustee, appoint at
summary: Section 97(1)(b) requires the investment manager, in consultation with the trustee, to appoint a majority of the board of directors or governing board for SPVs structured as companies or LLPs.

content: in consultation with the trustee, appoint at least such number of nominees on the board of directors or the governing board of such SPVs, which is in proportion to the shareholding or holding interest of the REIT in the SPV, as applicable; (c) the investment manager shall ensure that in every
summary: For REITs, Section 97(1)(b) mandates the appointment of board nominees in proportion to the REIT's shareholding or holding interest in the SPV.

content: the investment manager shall ensure that in every meeting, including annual general meeting of the SPV, the vote of the Investment Trust is exercised
summary: Section 97(1)(c) requires the investment manager to ensure the Investment Trust's vote is exercised in every meeting, including annual general meetings, of the SPV.

content: (2) The Investment Trust may invest in infrastructure projects or properties, as the case may be, through holdco, subject to the following: (a) the ultimate holding interest of the Investment Trust in the underlying SPV(s) is not less than twenty-six per cent (26%); (b) no other shareholder or
summary: Section 97(2) allows Investment Trusts to invest via a holdco, provided the Trust maintains an ultimate holding interest of at least 26% in the underlying SPV(s).

content: per cent (26%); (b) no other shareholder or partner of the holdco or the SPV(s) shall exercise any rights that prevent the Investment Trust, the holdco or the SPV(s) from complying with the regulatory requirements specified by Authority and recognised stock exchange(s) and an agreement has been
summary: Section 97(2)(b) prohibits shareholders or partners of a holdco or SPV from exercising rights that impede the Investment Trust's compliance with Authority and stock exchange regulatory requirements.

content: stock exchange(s) and an agreement has been entered into with such shareholders or partners to that effect prior to investment in the holdco and/or SPVs: Provided that the shareholders’ agreement or partnership agreement shall provide for an appropriate mechanism for resolution of disputes between
summary: Section 97 requires Investment Trusts to enter into shareholder or partnership agreements for holdcos and SPVs prior to investment, mandating the inclusion of formal dispute resolution mechanisms.

content: mechanism for resolution of disputes between the Investment Trust and the other shareholders or partners in the holdco and/or SPV; Provided further in case of inconsistencies between such agreement(s) and the obligations cast upon an Investment Trust by the Authority and recognised stock exchange,
summary: Section 97 stipulates that in the event of inconsistencies between shareholder/partnership agreements and the regulatory obligations imposed by the Authority or recognized stock exchanges, the regulatory obligations shall prevail.

content: by the Authority and recognised stock exchange, such obligations shall prevail; (c) the investment manager shall, in consultation with the Trustee, appoint the majority of the Board of directors or governing board of the holdco and SPV(s): Provided that in case of a REIT, the investment manager, in
summary: Under Section 97, the investment manager, in consultation with the Trustee, is responsible for appointing the majority of the Board of directors or governing board for holdcos and SPVs.

content: in case of a REIT, the investment manager, in consultation with the trustee, shall appoint at least such number of nominees on the board of directors or the governing board of the holdco and/or the SPV, which are in proportion to the shareholding or holding interest of the REIT in the SPV; and (d)
summary: For REITs, Section 97 mandates that the investment manager must appoint nominees to the board of directors or governing board of holdcos and SPVs in proportion to the REIT's shareholding or holding interest.

content: holding interest of the REIT in the SPV; and (d) the investment manager shall ensure that in every meeting including, annual general meeting of the Holdco and SPV(s), the vote of the Investment Trust is exercised
summary: Section 97 requires the investment manager to ensure that the Investment Trust's voting rights are exercised at all meetings, including annual general meetings, of the holdco and SPVs.

content: (3) The investments by an Investment Trust may be in IFSC or India or in foreign jurisdiction: Provided that where an investment is made in a foreign infrastructure project or real estate asset, as the case may be, the investment manager shall ensure that such investment complies with all the
summary: Section 97(3) permits Investment Trust investments in IFSC, India, or foreign jurisdictions, provided that investments in foreign infrastructure or real estate assets comply with all local laws and requirements.

content: ensure that such investment complies with all the applicable laws and requirements in such jurisdictions
summary: Section 97 mandates that all investments made by an Investment Trust in foreign jurisdictions must strictly adhere to the applicable laws and regulatory requirements of those specific jurisdictions.

content: (4) An Investment Trust shall hold completed and rent generating property or an infrastructure asset, as the case may be, for a period not less than three years from the date of purchase of such asset, directly or through holdco and/or SPV: Provided that this shall not apply to investment in
summary: Section 97(4) requires Investment Trusts to hold completed, rent-generating property or infrastructure assets for a minimum of three years from the date of purchase, whether held directly or through a holdco/SPV.

content: that this shall not apply to investment in securities of companies in infrastructure sector other than SPVs
summary: The three-year mandatory holding period for assets under Section 97(4) does not apply to investments in securities of companies within the infrastructure sector that are not classified as SPVs.

content: (5) For any sale of property, whether by the Investment Trust or holdco or the SPV or for sale of shares or interest in the SPV by the holdco or Investment Trust exceeding ten per cent
summary: Section 97(5) governs the sale of property or the sale of shares/interest in an SPV by an Investment Trust, holdco, or SPV when such transactions exceed ten percent of the asset or interest.

content: (10%) of the value of Investment Trust assets in a financial year, the Investment manager shall obtain prior approval from the unit holders. (6) An Investment Trust shall not invest in units of other Investment Trust(s)
summary: Section 97(10%) requires Investment Manager to obtain prior unit holder approval for specific asset value thresholds and prohibits Investment Trusts from investing in units of other Investment Trusts.

content: (7) An Investment Trust shall not lend to any person other than the holdco or special purpose vehicle(s) in which the Investment Trust has invested: Explanation.- Investments in debt securities shall not be considered as lending.
summary: Section 97(7) restricts Investment Trust lending exclusively to its holdco or special purpose vehicles (SPVs), explicitly excluding investments in debt securities from the definition of lending.

content: (1) The investment by an InvIT shall only be in holdco and/ or SPVs or infrastructure projects or securities in accordance with the requirements specified in these regulations and the investment strategy as detailed in the offer document: Provided that in case of PPP projects, the InvIT shall
summary: Section 98(1) mandates that InvIT investments be limited to holdcos, SPVs, infrastructure projects, or securities as defined by regulations and the offer document strategy.

content: that in case of PPP projects, the InvIT shall mandatorily invest in the infrastructure projects through holdco and/ or SPV
summary: Section 98 mandates that for Public-Private Partnership (PPP) projects, an InvIT must invest in infrastructure projects exclusively through a holdco or SPV.

content: (2) In case of Public Issues of InvIT:- (a) At least eighty per cent. (80%) of the value of the InvIT assets shall be invested, proportionate to the holding of the InvITs, in completed and revenue generating infrastructure projects
summary: Section 98(2)(a) requires that at least 80% of the value of InvIT assets in public issues be invested in completed and revenue-generating infrastructure projects, proportionate to the InvIT's holding.

content: Explanation I- If the investment has been made through a holdco and/ or SPV(s), only the portion of direct investments in completed and revenue generating projects by such holdco and/ or SPV(s) shall be considered under this clause, and the remaining portion shall be included in clause (b) of this
summary: Explanation I to Section 98(2) clarifies that for investments via holdco or SPV, only the portion directly invested in completed and revenue-generating projects counts toward the 80% requirement, with the remainder allocated to clause (b).

content: portion shall be included in clause (b) of this sub-regulation
summary: This provision specifies that the remaining portion of InvIT investments not qualifying as completed and revenue-generating projects under Section 98(2)(a) must be categorized under clause (b).

content: Explanation II- If any project is implemented in stages, the part of the project which can be categorised as completed and revenue generating project shall be considered under this clause and the remaining portion shall be included under clause (b) of this sub-regulation; (b) Not more than twenty
summary: Explanation II to Section 98(2) mandates that for phased infrastructure projects, only the completed and revenue-generating portion qualifies under clause (a), while the remaining portion falls under clause (b).

content: of this sub-regulation; (b) Not more than twenty per cent
summary: This provision establishes the 20% limit for InvIT asset allocation under clause (b) of Section 98(2).

content: (20%) of value of the InvIT assets shall be invested in: (i) under-construction infrastructure projects, whether directly or through holdco and/ or SPVs: Provided that the investment in such assets shall not exceed ten per cent
summary: Section 98(2)(b)(i) limits investment in under-construction infrastructure projects to 20% of InvIT asset value, with a specific sub-cap of 10% for certain assets.

content: (10%) of the value of the InvIT assets; (ii) listed or unlisted debt of companies or body corporate in infrastructure sector: Provided that this investment shall not include any investment made in debt of the holdco and/ or SPV(s); (iii) equity shares of listed companies which derive not less than
summary: Section 98 permits InvIT investments in up to 10% of InvIT asset value in infrastructure sector debt (excluding holdco/SPV debt) and equity shares of specific listed companies.

content: of listed companies which derive not less than eighty per cent
summary: Section 98 specifies that eligible equity investments must be in listed companies deriving at least 80% of their operating income from the infrastructure sector.

content: (80%) of their operating income from infrastructure sector; (iv) government securities; (v) securities issued by a supranational agency; and (vi) money market instruments, liquid mutual funds or cash equivalents
summary: Section 98 authorizes InvIT investments in government securities, supranational agency securities, money market instruments, liquid mutual funds, and cash equivalents.

content: (3) In respect of InvITs having raised funding through public issue, if the investment conditions are breached on account of market movements of the price of the underlying assets or securities, the investment manager shall inform the same to the trustee and ensure that the investment conditions as
summary: For publicly issued InvITs, Section 98 requires the investment manager to notify the trustee if market movements cause a breach of investment conditions.

content: and ensure that the investment conditions as specified in these regulations are satisfied within six (6) months of such breach: Provided that the period may be extended to one year subject to approval from investors
summary: Section 98 mandates that InvITs rectify investment condition breaches within six months, extendable to one year with investor approval.

content: (4) In case of private placement of InvIT:- (a) At least eighty per cent
summary: Section 98 requires privately placed InvITs to maintain at least 80% of their asset value in eligible infrastructure projects, held directly or through holdcos/SPVs.

content: (80%) of the value of the InvIT assets shall be invested in eligible infrastructure projects either directly or through holdcos or through SPVs; (b) Un-invested funds may be invested in: (i) listed or unlisted debt of companies or body corporate in infrastructure sector: Provided that this shall
summary: Section 98 dictates that un-invested funds for private InvITs may be placed in infrastructure sector debt, excluding debt issued by the holdco or SPVs.

content: infrastructure sector: Provided that this shall not include any investment made in debt of the holdco and/ or SPV(s); (ii) equity shares of listed companies which derive not less than eighty per cent
summary: Section 98 restricts private InvIT un-invested fund allocations to infrastructure debt and equity shares of listed companies deriving at least 80% of operating income from infrastructure.

content: (80%) of their operating income from infrastructure sector; (iii) government securities; (iv) securities issued by a supranational agency; and (v) money market instruments, liquid mutual funds or cash equivalents.
summary: Section 98 permits private InvITs to hold un-invested funds in government securities, supranational agency securities, money market instruments, liquid mutual funds, or cash equivalents.

content: (1) The investment by a REIT shall be either directly or through an SPV or through holdco
summary: Section 99 mandates that REIT investments must be executed either directly or through a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV) or a holdco.

content: (2) A REIT shall invest only in the following:- (a) real estate, whether freehold or leasehold, in or outside IFSC; (b) real estate related assets; (c) listed or unlisted debt securities or listed shares of local or foreign non-property corporations; (d) government securities; (e) securities issued
summary: Section 99(2) mandates that a REIT must restrict its investments to freehold or leasehold real estate, real estate related assets, debt securities, listed shares of non-property corporations, and government securities.

content: (d) government securities; (e) securities issued by a supranational agency; and (f) cash and cash-equivalent items
summary: Section 99(2) further permits REIT investments in securities issued by supranational agencies and cash or cash-equivalent items.

content: (3) The investments in case of public offer should be in compliance with the following requirements: (a) at least seventy-five per cent
summary: Section 99(3) establishes that for public offers, a minimum of 75% of REIT assets must be invested in income-producing real estate.

content: (75%) of the REIT assets should be invested in income-producing real estate; (b) should not undertake property development activities, unless it intends to hold the developed property upon completion; (c) should not invest in vacant land and mortgages except for mortgage-backed securities: Provided
summary: REITs are prohibited from undertaking property development unless the property is held upon completion and are restricted from investing in vacant land or mortgages, excluding mortgage-backed securities.

content: except for mortgage-backed securities: Provided that this requirement shall not apply to any land which is contiguous and extension of an existing project being implemented in stages; (d) the total contract value of property development activities undertaken and investments in uncompleted property
summary: The restriction on vacant land investments under Section 99(3)(c) does not apply to land that is contiguous to and an extension of an existing project implemented in stages.

content: and investments in uncompleted property developments should not exceed ten per cent
summary: Section 99(3)(d) limits the total contract value of property development activities and investments in uncompleted property developments to 10% of total REIT assets.

content: (10%) of the REIT assets: Provided that the total contract value of property development activities may exceed ten per cent. (10%) subject to a maximum of twenty five per cent. (25%) of the REIT assets only if: (i) the additional allowance of up to fifteen per cent
summary: The 10% limit on property development and uncompleted projects may be increased to a maximum of 25% of REIT assets under specific conditions.

content: (15%) of the REIT assets is utilised solely for the redevelopment of an existing property that has been held by it for at least three (3) years and which the REIT will continue to hold for at least three (3) years after the completion of the redevelopment; and (ii) it has obtained specific approval
summary: An additional 15% allowance for property development is permitted solely for the redevelopment of existing properties held for at least three years, provided the REIT retains the property for three years post-completion.

content: and (ii) it has obtained specific approval of the unit holders
summary: Increasing the property development limit beyond 10% requires obtaining specific approval from the REIT unit holders.

content: (e) for investments in debt securities or listed shares of local or foreign non-property corporations, or government securities or securities issued by a supranational agency, or cash or cash-equivalent items, not more than five per cent
summary: Section 99(3)(e) caps investments in debt securities, listed shares of non-property corporations, government securities, supranational agency securities, and cash equivalents at 5% of REIT assets.

content: (5%) of the REIT assets can be invested in any one issuer’s securities or any one manager’s funds; and (f) should not derive more than ten per cent. (10%) of its revenue from sources other than: i
summary: Section 99 limits REIT asset investment in any single issuer's securities or manager's funds to 5% and restricts non-rental revenue sources to a maximum of 10%.

content: (10%) of its revenue from sources other than: i. rental payments from the tenants of the real estate held by it, or Explanation.- rental payments include income that is ancillary or incidental to the leasing of real estate such as income from use of signage space and advertising contributions by
summary: Under Section 99, REIT revenue must be derived primarily from rental payments, which include ancillary income from signage space and advertising contributions by tenants.

content: of signage space and advertising contributions by tenants; ii
summary: Section 99 includes income from signage space and advertising contributions by tenants as permissible ancillary rental income for REITs.

content: interest, dividends and other similar payments from permissible investments. (4) The investment restrictions and requirements under clauses (d) and (e) of sub-regulation (3) are applicable at the time of entering into the transactions
summary: Section 99 permits revenue from interest, dividends, and similar payments from permissible investments, with investment restrictions under sub-regulation (3) clauses (d) and (e) applying at the time of transaction.

content: (5) If the investment conditions specified under clauses (a) and (f) of sub-regulation (3) are breached on account of market movements of the price of the underlying assets or securities or change in tenants or expiry of lease or sale of properties, the investment manager shall inform the same to
summary: If Section 99(3) investment conditions are breached due to market movements, lease expirations, or property sales, the investment manager must notify the trustee.

content: the investment manager shall inform the same to the trustee and ensure that the conditions as specified in these regulations are satisfied within six (6) months of such breach: Provided that the period may be extended by another six (6) months subject to approval from unit holders
summary: Following a breach of investment conditions, the investment manager must restore compliance within six months, extendable by another six months with unit holder approval.

content: (6) The investments in case of private placement should be in compliance with the following requirements:- (a) At least eighty per cent
summary: Section 99(6) mandates that for private placements, at least 80% of REIT asset value must be invested in eligible real estate properties directly or through holdcos or SPVs.

content: (80%) of the value of the REIT assets should be invested in eligible real estate properties either directly or through holdcos or through SPVs; (b) Un-invested funds may be invested in: (i) listed or unlisted debt of companies or body corporate in real estate sector: Provided that this shall not
summary: Section 99(6) requires 80% of REIT assets to be in real estate properties and permits un-invested funds to be placed in debt of real estate companies, excluding debt of holdcos or SPVs.

content: real estate sector: Provided that this shall not include any investment made in debt of the holdco and/ or SPV(s); (ii) equity shares of listed companies which derive not less than eighty per cent
summary: Un-invested REIT funds may be placed in equity shares of listed companies that derive at least 80% of their operating income from the real estate sector.

content: (80%) of their operating income from real estate sector; (iii) government securities; (iv) securities issued by a supranational agency; and (v) money market instruments, liquid mutual funds or cash equivalents.
summary: Section 99 permits un-invested REIT funds to be held in government securities, securities of supranational agencies, money market instruments, liquid mutual funds, or cash equivalents.

content: (1) With respect to distributions made by the Investment Trust and the holdco and/or SPV,- (a) not less than ninety per cent
summary: Section 100(1) establishes the mandatory distribution requirements for Investment Trusts, holdcos, and Special Purpose Vehicles (SPVs).

content: (90%) of net distributable cash flows of the SPV shall be distributed to the Investment Trust /holdco in proportion of its holding in the SPV; (b) not less than ninety per cent
summary: SPVs must distribute at least 90% of their net distributable cash flows to the Investment Trust or holdco proportional to their respective holdings.

content: (90%) of net distributable cash flows of the Investment Trust shall be distributed to the unit holders; (c) with regard to distribution of net distributable cash flows by the holdco to the Investment Trust, the following shall be complied: (i) with respect to the cash flows received by the holdco
summary: Investment Trusts are required to distribute at least 90% of their net distributable cash flows to unit holders.

content: respect to the cash flows received by the holdco from underlying SPVs, hundred per cent
summary: Holdcos must distribute 100% of cash flows received from underlying SPVs to the Investment Trust.

content: (100%) of such cash flows received by the holdco shall be distributed to the Investment Trust; and (ii) with respect to the cash flows generated by the holdco on its own, not less than ninety per cent
summary: Holdcos must distribute at least 90% of net distributable cash flows generated independently to the Investment Trust.

content: (90%) of such net distributable cash flows shall be distributed by the holdco to the Investment Trust
summary: Holdcos are mandated to distribute at least 90% of their net distributable cash flows to the Investment Trust.

content: (d) such distributions shall be declared and made not less than once every six (6) months in every financial year in case of publicly offered InvITs and shall be made not later than fifteen (15) days from the date of such declaration
summary: Publicly offered InvITs must declare and make distributions at least once every six months, with payments due within 15 days of declaration.

content: (2) If any infrastructure asset or property, as the case may be, is sold by the Investment Trust or holdco or SPV or if the equity shares or interest in the holdco/ SPV are sold by the Investment Trust: (a) If the Investment Trust proposes to re-invest the sale proceeds into infrastructure asset or
summary: Section 100(2) governs the treatment of sale proceeds from infrastructure assets, properties, or equity interests in holdcos/SPVs by the Investment Trust, holdco, or SPV.

content: the sale proceeds into infrastructure asset or another property, as the case may be, it shall not be required to distribute any sales proceeds to the investors; (b) If the Investment Trust proposes not to invest the sales proceeds into any other infrastructure asset or property, as the case may be,
summary: Investment Trusts are exempt from distributing sale proceeds to investors if they intend to reinvest those proceeds into other infrastructure assets or properties.

content: asset or property, as the case may be, within a period of one (1) year, it shall be required to distribute the same in accordance with sub-regulation (1).
summary: If sale proceeds are not reinvested into infrastructure assets or properties within one year, the Investment Trust must distribute them according to the requirements of sub-regulation (1).

content: (1) The following provisions shall apply to an Investment Trust listed on a recognised stock exchange: (a) An Investment Trust whose units are listed on a recognised stock exchange may issue debt securities in the manner specified by the Authority: Provided that such debt securities shall be listed
summary: Section 101(1)(a) mandates that Investment Trusts listed on a recognised stock exchange may issue debt securities as specified by the Authority, provided such securities are listed on a recognised stock exchange.

content: that such debt securities shall be listed on recognised stock exchange(s)
summary: Section 101(1)(a) requires that any debt securities issued by a listed Investment Trust must be listed on a recognised stock exchange.

content: (b) The aggregate consolidated borrowings and deferred payments of the Investment Trust, holdco and the SPV(s), net of cash and cash equivalents shall not exceed: (i) seventy per cent. (70%) of the value of the InvIT assets; or (ii) forty nine per cent. (49%) of the value of the REIT assets
summary: Section 101(1)(b) limits the aggregate consolidated borrowings and deferred payments of an Investment Trust, holdco, and SPV(s), net of cash, to 70% of InvIT assets or 49% of REIT assets.

content: (49%) of the value of the REIT assets. (c) If the aggregate consolidated borrowings and deferred payments of the Investment Trust, holdco and the SPV(s), net of cash and cash equivalents exceed twenty five per cent
summary: Section 101(1)(c) establishes a threshold of 25% of asset value for aggregate consolidated borrowings and deferred payments of an Investment Trust, holdco, and SPV(s) before additional regulatory requirements apply.

content: (25%) of the value of the assets of Investment Trust, for any further borrowing,– (i) up to forty nine per cent. (49%), the Investment Trust shall - (a) obtain credit rating from a credit rating agency; and (b) seek approval of unitholders. (ii) above forty–nine per cent
summary: For borrowings between 25% and 49% of asset value, Section 101(1)(c)(i) requires the Investment Trust to obtain a credit rating from a credit rating agency and secure unitholder approval.

content: (ii) above forty–nine per cent. (49%), an InvIT shall - (a) obtain a credit rating of “AAA” or equivalent for its consolidated borrowing and the proposed borrowing, from a credit rating agency; (b) utilize the funds only for acquisition or development of infrastructure projects; (c) have a track
summary: Section 101(1)(c)(ii) mandates that InvITs borrowing above 49% must obtain a 'AAA' credit rating and restrict fund utilization exclusively to the acquisition or development of infrastructure projects.

content: of infrastructure projects; (c) have a track record of at least six distributions, on a continuous basis, post listing, in the years preceding the financial year in which the enhanced borrowings are proposed to be made; and (d) obtain prior approval of unitholders
summary: To exceed 49% borrowing, an InvIT must demonstrate a track record of at least six continuous distributions post-listing and obtain prior approval from unitholders per Section 101(1)(c)(ii).

content: (d) If the conditions specified in the above sub-clauses are breached on account of market movements of the price of the underlying assets or securities, the investment manager shall inform the same to the trustee and ensure that the conditions are satisfied within six months of such breach
summary: Section 101(1)(d) requires the investment manager to notify the trustee and rectify any borrowing limit breaches caused by market movements within six months.

content: (2) An Investment Trust whose units are not listed on a recognised stock exchange may undertake borrowing to the extent permitted under the trust deed, after seeking approval from such number of investors as specified in the trust deed.
summary: Section 101(2) permits unlisted Investment Trusts to borrow according to their trust deed provisions, subject to approval from the number of investors specified therein.

content: (1) A full valuation shall be conducted by the valuer at least once in every financial year: Provided that such full valuation shall be conducted at the end of the financial year ending March 31 st within two (2) months from the end of such year
summary: Section 102(1) mandates a full valuation by a valuer at least once per financial year, to be completed within two months of the March 31st financial year-end.

content: (2) A half yearly valuation of the assets of Investment Trust shall be conducted by the valuer for the half-year ending on September 30 for incorporating any key changes in the previous six (6) months and such half yearly valuation report shall be prepared within one (1) month from the date of end
summary: Section 102(2) mandates a half-yearly valuation of Investment Trust assets by a valuer for the period ending September 30, with the report due within one month of the period's end.

content: within one (1) month from the date of end of such half year: Provided that in case the consolidated borrowings and deferred payments of an InvIT is above forty-nine per cent
summary: Section 102 establishes that if an InvIT's consolidated borrowings and deferred payments exceed 49%, the standard half-yearly valuation frequency is superseded by more frequent requirements.

content: (49%), the valuation of the assets of such InvIT shall be conducted by the valuer for quarter ending June, September and December, for incorporating any key changes in the previous quarter and such quarterly report shall be prepared within one month from the date of the end of such quarter
summary: For InvITs with consolidated borrowings exceeding 49%, the valuer must conduct quarterly valuations for periods ending June, September, and December, submitting reports within one month of each quarter's end.

content: (3) Valuation reports received by the investment manager shall be submitted to the recognised stock exchange(s) within fifteen (15) days from the receipt of such valuation reports, in respect of an InvIT whose units are listed on a recognised stock exchange
summary: Section 102(3) requires the investment manager to submit received valuation reports to the recognized stock exchange(s) within 15 days of receipt for listed InvIT units.

content: (4) Prior to any issue of units by publicly offered Investment Trust other than bonus issue, the valuer shall undertake full valuation of all the assets and include the same in the Offer Document: Provided that such valuation report shall not be more than six months old at the time of such offer:
summary: Section 102(4) mandates a full asset valuation by the valuer prior to any public issue of InvIT units, excluding bonus issues, provided the report is not older than six months.

content: than six months old at the time of such offer: Provided further that this shall not apply in cases where full valuation has been undertaken not more than six (6) months prior to such issue and no material changes have occurred thereafter
summary: The requirement for a full valuation prior to a public issue of units is waived if a full valuation was conducted within the preceding six months and no material changes have occurred.

content: (5) For any transaction of purchase or sale of infrastructure projects or properties, whether directly or through holdco and/or SPVs, for publicly offered Investment Trusts, a full valuation of the specific project shall be undertaken by the valuer; if,– (i) in case of a purchase transaction, the
summary: Section 102(5) requires a full valuation by the valuer for any purchase or sale of infrastructure projects or properties by publicly offered Investment Trusts, including transactions through holdcos or SPVs.

content: if,– (i) in case of a purchase transaction, the asset is proposed to be purchased at a value greater than one hundred and ten per cent
summary: Under Section 102(5)(i), a full valuation is triggered if a purchase transaction for an infrastructure project or property exceeds 110% of the valuer's assessed value.

content: (110%) of the value of the asset as assessed by the valuer; (ii) in case of a sale transaction, the asset is proposed to be sold at a value less than ninety per cent. (90%) of the value of the asset as assessed by the valuer, approval of the unit holders shall be obtained
summary: Section 102(5) mandates unit holder approval if an asset is purchased above 110% or sold below 90% of the valuer's assessed value.

content: (6) In case of any material development that may have an impact on the valuation of the assets under Investment Trust, then investment manager shall require the valuer to undertake full valuation of the infrastructure project or property under consideration within not more than two (2) months from
summary: Section 102(6) requires the investment manager to commission a full valuation of an infrastructure project or property within two months if a material development impacts the asset's valuation.

content: within not more than two (2) months from the date of such event and disclose the same to the trustee and the recognised stock exchange(s) within fifteen (15) days of such valuation
summary: Section 102 mandates that valuation disclosures must be submitted to the trustee and recognized stock exchange(s) within fifteen days of the valuation, following an event occurring within a maximum of two months.

content: (7) The valuer shall not value any assets in which it has either been involved with the acquisition or disposal within the last twelve (12) months other than such cases where valuer was engaged by the Investment Trust for such acquisition or disposal.
summary: Under Section 102(7), a valuer is prohibited from valuing assets involved in their own acquisition or disposal within the preceding twelve months, unless the valuer was specifically engaged by the Investment Trust for that transaction.

content: (1) An Investment Trust whose units are not listed on a recognised stock exchange may choose to surrender its certificate of registration to the Authority and on acceptance of surrender of certificate of registration, it shall no longer undertake the activity of an Investment Trust
summary: Section 103(1) permits an unlisted Investment Trust to surrender its certificate of registration to the Authority, upon which it must cease all Investment Trust activities.

content: (2) The Investment Trust and parties to the Investment Trust shall continue to be liable for all their acts of omissions and commissions with respect to activities of the Investment Trust notwithstanding surrender of registration to the Authority.
summary: Section 103(2) stipulates that an Investment Trust and its associated parties remain liable for all acts, omissions, and commissions related to their activities even after the surrender of registration.

content: (1) A Family Investment Fund may be set up in the IFSC as a Company, Trust only in the form of Contributory Trust or Limited Liability Partnership or any other form as may be permitted by the Authority from time to time
summary: Section 104(1) authorizes the establishment of a Family Investment Fund in the IFSC as a Company, Contributory Trust, Limited Liability Partnership, or other forms permitted by the Authority.

content: Provided where the contributory vehicle is in the form of a Trust, it shall ensure that, - (a) the beneficiaries are identifiable based on the provisions of the Trust deed, though not specifically named in the Trust deed; (b) the share of each beneficiary should be capable of being determined based
summary: Section 104 requires that for a Family Investment Fund structured as a Trust, beneficiaries must be identifiable via the Trust deed and their respective shares must be determinable.

content: should be capable of being determined based on the provisions mentioned in the trust deed and shall not be at the discretion of the trustee; and (c) further contributors to the Trust, in addition to the initial contributors, shall not make the existing beneficiaries unknown or their shares
summary: Section 104 mandates that beneficiary shares in a Trust-based Family Investment Fund must be fixed by the Trust deed rather than trustee discretion, and additional contributors must not obscure beneficiary identity or share proportions.

content: existing beneficiaries unknown or their shares indeterminate
summary: Section 104 prohibits the addition of new contributors to a Family Investment Fund Trust if such additions render existing beneficiaries unknown or their respective shares indeterminate.

content: (2) The requirements under regulations 5 and 8 pertaining to Legal form and Net-worth of a FME respectively, shall not be applicable to a Family Investment Fund
summary: Section 104(2) exempts Family Investment Funds from the legal form and net-worth requirements otherwise applicable to a Fund Management Entity (FME) under regulations 5 and 8.

content: (3) A Family Investment Fund should have and maintain a minimum investment of USD 10 million within a period of three (3) years from the date of obtaining certificate of registration
summary: Section 104(3) requires a Family Investment Fund to maintain a minimum investment of USD 10 million, to be achieved within three years of receiving its certificate of registration.

content: (4) The Family Investment Fund may either be open-ended or close-ended, depending upon the requirements of the family
summary: Section 104(4) stipulates that a Family Investment Fund may be structured as either an open-ended or close-ended fund based on family requirements.

content: (5) A Family Investment Fund may set up additional investment vehicles after filing documents for such vehicles with the Authority and payment of applicable fee as specified by the Authority
summary: Section 104(5) permits a Family Investment Fund to establish additional investment vehicles upon filing necessary documentation with the Authority and paying the prescribed fees.

content: Explanation I.- Such additional investment vehicles in the form of companies, limited liability partnerships, trusts or any other form as may be specified by the Authority, shall be considered in conjunction for the purpose of meeting the condition specified under sub-regulation (3)
summary: Explanation I to Section 104 clarifies that additional investment vehicles, including companies, LLPs, or trusts, are aggregated to satisfy the eligibility conditions under sub-regulation (3).

content: Explanation II. - The Family Investment Fund or the investment vehicle may be construed as Category I Alternative Investment Fund, Category II Alternative Investment Fund or Category III Alternative Investment Fund depending on the investment strategy adopted in accordance with regulation 30.
summary: Explanation II to Section 104 provides that a Family Investment Fund or its vehicles may be classified as Category I, II, or III Alternative Investment Funds based on investment strategies defined in regulation 30.

content: A Family Investment Fund may undertake all activities related to managing family investment fund and such other activities as may be specified by the Authority.
summary: Section 105 authorizes a Family Investment Fund to perform management activities and other functions as specified by the Authority.

content: Subject to other provisions of these regulations, a Family Investment Fund may invest monies only in the following instruments or entities in IFSC, India or foreign jurisdictions in the manner and to the extent as specified by the Authority:- (a) Unlisted securities; (b) Securities listed or to be
summary: Section 106 restricts Family Investment Fund investments to specific instruments or entities in IFSC, India, or foreign jurisdictions, including unlisted and listed securities.

content: securities; (b) Securities listed or to be listed or traded on stock exchanges; (c) Money market instruments; (d) Debt securities; (e) Securitised debt instruments, which are either asset backed or mortgage-backed securities; (f) Units of investment schemes; (g) Derivatives including commodity
summary: Section 106 permits investments in money market instruments, debt securities, securitised debt instruments, units of investment schemes, and derivatives.

content: schemes; (g) Derivatives including commodity derivatives; (h) Limited Liability Partnerships; (i) Physical assets such as real estate, bullion, art, etc.; or (j) Such other securities or financial product/assets or instruments as specified by the Authority.
summary: Section 106 allows Family Investment Fund investments in commodity derivatives, Limited Liability Partnerships, physical assets like real estate, bullion, and art, or other assets specified by the Authority.

content: A Family Investment Fund may borrow funds and engage in leveraging activities as per their risk management policy.
summary: Section 107 grants a Family Investment Fund the authority to borrow funds and engage in leveraging activities in accordance with its risk management policy.

content: A FME may launch a scheme on behalf of a third-party in accordance with the provisions of this Part.
summary: Section 107A authorizes a Fund Management Entity (FME) to launch investment schemes on behalf of a third party in compliance with the applicable regulatory provisions.

metadata.contained_chapter: PART D: THIRD-PARTY FUND MANAGEMENT SERVICES

content: For the purpose of this Part: i. “third-party fund management services” or “third-party fund management arrangement” refers to the activity wherein a Registered FME manages the schemes on behalf of a third- party; ii
summary: Section 107B defines 'third-party fund management services' or 'third-party fund management arrangement' as the activity where a Registered FME manages schemes on behalf of a third party.

metadata.contained_chapter: PART D: THIRD-PARTY FUND MANAGEMENT SERVICES

content: “third-party fund manager” means and includes an entity registered or regulated, for the purposes of fund management, portfolio management, investment advisory or any other similar activity, by whatever name called, with the concerned financial sector regulator in the country of its incorporation,
summary: Section 107B defines a 'third-party fund manager' as an entity regulated for fund management, portfolio management, or investment advisory by the financial sector regulator in its country of incorporation.

metadata.contained_chapter: PART D: THIRD-PARTY FUND MANAGEMENT SERVICES

content: regulator in the country of its incorporation, and which avails the third-party fund management services from a Registered FME.
summary: Section 107B specifies that a 'third-party fund manager' must be an entity that avails third-party fund management services from a Registered FME.

metadata.contained_chapter: PART D: THIRD-PARTY FUND MANAGEMENT SERVICES

content: (1) A FME intending to set up and manage schemes on behalf of a third-party shall seek authorisation from the Authority under this Part for undertaking third-party fund management services in accordance with the terms and conditions specified in this Part and shall comply with other conditions as
summary: Section 107C(1) mandates that an FME must obtain authorization from the Authority to set up and manage schemes on behalf of a third party, subject to specified terms and conditions.

metadata.contained_chapter: PART D: THIRD-PARTY FUND MANAGEMENT SERVICES

content: Part and shall comply with other conditions as may be specified by the Authority from time to time
